title: Goodness-of-fit measures to compare observed and simulated time series with hydroGOF
text: following examples use well-known Nash-Sutcliffe efficiency (NSE), can repeat computations using goodness--fit measures included hydroGOF package (e.g., KGE, ubRMSE, dr).

title: Goodness-of-fit measures to compare observed and simulated time series with hydroGOF
text: NSE simulated values equal observations plus random noise first half observed values. random noise relative importance low flows medium high flows. Randomly changing first 1826 elements ‘sim’, using normal distribution mean 10 standard deviation equal 1 (default ‘rnorm’).  Let’s look goodness--fit measures:
code: sim[1:1826] <- obs[1:1826] + rnorm(1826, mean=10) ggof(sim, obs) NSE(sim=sim, obs=obs) ## [1] 0.8739885 mNSE(sim=sim, obs=obs)               # modified NSE ## [1] 0.6049584 rNSE(sim=sim, obs=obs)               # relative NSE ## [1] -0.5687206 KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs)                # Kling-Gupta efficiency (KGE), 2009 ## [1] -0.3169356 KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2012") # Kling-Gupta efficiency (KGE), 2012 ## [1] -0.3338167 KGElf(sim=sim, obs=obs)              # KGE for low flows ## [1] -0.07585665 KGEnp(sim=sim, obs=obs)              # Non-parametric KGE ## [1] 0.6340134 sKGE(sim=sim, obs=obs)               # Split KGE ## [1] -0.3452463 d(sim=sim, obs=obs)                  # Index of agreement (d) ## [1] 0.9697286 rd(sim=sim, obs=obs)                 # Relative d ## [1] 0.6231506 md(sim=sim, obs=obs)                 # Modified d ## [1] 0.7980307 dr(sim=sim, obs=obs)                 # Refined d ## [1] 0.8024792 VE(sim=sim, obs=obs)                 # Volumetric efficiency ## [1] 0.6838531 cp(sim=sim, obs=obs)                 # Coefficient of persistence ## [1] 0.4683536 pbias(sim=sim, obs=obs)              # Percent bias (PBIAS) ## [1] 31.6 pbiasfdc(sim=sim, obs=obs)           # PBIAS in the slope of the midsegment of the FDC ## [Note: 'thr.shw' was set to FALSE to avoid confusing legends...] ## [1] -34.95419 rmse(sim=sim, obs=obs)               # Root mean square error (RMSE) ## [1] 7.104063 ubRMSE(sim=sim, obs=obs)             # Unbiased RMSE ## [1] 5.047547 rPearson(sim=sim, obs=obs)           # Pearson correlation coefficient ## [1] 0.9698058 rSpearman(sim=sim, obs=obs)          # Spearman rank correlation coefficient ## [1] 0.8362479 R2(sim=sim, obs=obs)                 # Coefficient of determination (R2) ## [1] 0.8739885 br2(sim=sim, obs=obs)                # R2 multiplied by the slope of the regression line ## [1] 0.7780545

title: Goodness-of-fit measures to compare observed and simulated time series with hydroGOF
text: NSE simulated values equal observations plus random noise first half observed values applying (natural) logarithm ‘sim’ ‘obs’ computations. Verifying previous value: Let’s look goodness--fit measures:
code: NSE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log) ## [1] 0.4799297 lsim <- log(sim) lobs <- log(obs) NSE(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) ## [1] 0.4799297 mNSE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)               # modified NSE ## [1] 0.4822804 rNSE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)               # relative NSE ## [1] -4.560383 KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)                # Kling-Gupta efficiency (KGE), 2009 ## [1] -0.20655 KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2012", fun=log) # Kling-Gupta efficiency (KGE), 2012 ## [1] -0.2279992 KGElf(sim=sim, obs=obs)                       # KGE for low flows (it does not allow 'fun' argument) ## [1] -0.07585665 KGEnp(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)              # Non-parametric KGE ## [1] 0.7437751 sKGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)               # Split KGE ## [1] -0.4594372 d(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)                  # Index of agreement (d) ## [1] 0.8609788 rd(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)                 # Relative d ## [1] -0.4863585 md(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)                 # Modified d ## [1] 0.7385673 dr(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)                 # Refined d ## [1] 0.7411402 VE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)                 # Volumetric efficiency ## [1] 0.8124419 cp(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)                 # Coefficient of persistence ## [1] -7.948224 pbias(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)              # Percent bias (PBIAS) ## [1] 18.8 pbiasfdc(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)           # PBIAS in the slope of the midsegment of the FDC ## [Note: 'thr.shw' was set to FALSE to avoid confusing legends...] ## [1] -46.39001 rmse(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)               # Root mean square error (RMSE) ## [1] 0.6955452 ubRMSE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)             # Unbiased RMSE ## [1] 0.5520467 rPearson(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)           # Pearson correlation coefficient (r) ## [1] 0.8221915 rSpearman(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)          # Spearman rank correlation coefficient (rho) ## [1] 0.8362479 R2(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)                 # Coefficient of determination (R2) ## [1] 0.4799297 br2(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)                # R2 multiplied by the slope of the regression line ## [1] 0.4299904

title: Goodness-of-fit measures to compare observed and simulated time series with hydroGOF
text: NSE simulated values equal observations plus random noise first half observed values applying (natural) logarithm ‘sim’ ‘obs’ adding Pushpalatha2012 constant computations Verifying previous value, epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012: Let’s look goodness--fit measures:
code: NSE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="Pushpalatha2012") ## [1] 0.4871177 eps  <- mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE)/100 lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) NSE(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) ## [1] 0.4871177 gof(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="Pushpalatha2012", do.spearman=TRUE, do.pbfdc=TRUE) ##              [,1] ## ME           0.41 ## MAE          0.41 ## MSE          0.46 ## RMSE         0.68 ## ubRMSE       0.54 ## NRMSE %     71.60 ## PBIAS %     18.20 ## RSR          0.72 ## rSD          0.89 ## NSE          0.49 ## mNSE         0.48 ## rNSE        -2.08 ## wNSE         0.74 ## wsNSE        0.78 ## d            0.86 ## dr           0.74 ## md           0.74 ## rd           0.18 ## cp          -7.68 ## r            0.83 ## R2           0.49 ## bR2          0.44 ## VE           0.82 ## KGE         -0.20 ## KGElf       -0.08 ## KGEnp        0.74 ## KGEkm        0.74 ## sKGE        -0.39 ## APFB         0.01 ## HFB          0.98 ## rSpearman    0.84 ## pbiasFDC % -45.87

title: Goodness-of-fit measures to compare observed and simulated time series with hydroGOF
text: NSE simulated values equal observations plus random noise first half observed values applying (natural) logarithm ‘sim’ ‘obs’ adding user-defined constant computations Verifying previous value: Let’s look goodness--fit measures:
code: eps <- 0.01 NSE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherValue", epsilon.value=eps) ## [1] 0.4804 lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) NSE(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) ## [1] 0.4804 gof(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherValue", epsilon.value=eps, do.spearman=TRUE, do.pbfdc=TRUE) ##              [,1] ## ME           0.42 ## MAE          0.42 ## MSE          0.48 ## RMSE         0.69 ## ubRMSE       0.55 ## NRMSE %     72.10 ## PBIAS %     18.70 ## RSR          0.72 ## rSD          0.88 ## NSE          0.48 ## mNSE         0.48 ## rNSE        -4.22 ## wNSE         0.74 ## wsNSE        0.78 ## d            0.86 ## dr           0.74 ## md           0.74 ## rd          -0.39 ## cp          -7.93 ## r            0.82 ## R2           0.48 ## bR2          0.43 ## VE           0.81 ## KGE         -0.21 ## KGElf       -0.08 ## KGEnp        0.74 ## KGEkm        0.73 ## sKGE        -0.45 ## APFB         0.01 ## HFB          0.98 ## rSpearman    0.84 ## pbiasFDC % -46.36

title: Goodness-of-fit measures to compare observed and simulated time series with hydroGOF
text: NSE simulated values equal observations plus random noise first half observed values applying (natural) logarithm ‘sim’ ‘obs’ using user-defined factor multiply mean observed values obtain constant added ‘sim’ ‘obs’ computations Verifying previous value: Let’s look goodness--fit measures:
code: fact <- 1/50 NSE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherFactor", epsilon.value=fact) ## [1] 0.4938294 fact <- 1/50 eps  <- fact*mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE) lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) NSE(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) ## [1] 0.4938294 gof(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherFactor", epsilon.value=fact, do.spearman=TRUE, do.pbfdc=TRUE) ##              [,1] ## ME           0.41 ## MAE          0.41 ## MSE          0.44 ## RMSE         0.66 ## ubRMSE       0.52 ## NRMSE %     71.10 ## PBIAS %     17.60 ## RSR          0.71 ## rSD          0.89 ## NSE          0.49 ## mNSE         0.48 ## rNSE        -1.33 ## wNSE         0.74 ## wsNSE        0.78 ## d            0.87 ## dr           0.74 ## md           0.74 ## rd           0.38 ## cp          -7.43 ## r            0.83 ## R2           0.49 ## bR2          0.44 ## VE           0.82 ## KGE         -0.19 ## KGElf       -0.07 ## KGEnp        0.74 ## KGEkm        0.75 ## sKGE        -0.35 ## APFB         0.01 ## HFB          0.98 ## rSpearman    0.84 ## pbiasFDC % -45.37

title: Goodness-of-fit measures to compare observed and simulated time series with hydroGOF
text: NSE simulated values equal observations plus random noise first half observed values applying user-defined function ‘sim’ ‘obs’ computations: Verifying previous value, epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012:
code: fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)} NSE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1) ## [1] 0.7265255 sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) NSE(sim=sim1, obs=obs1) ## [1] 0.7265255 gof(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1, do.spearman=TRUE, do.pbfdc=TRUE) ##              [,1] ## ME           0.65 ## MAE          0.65 ## MSE          0.92 ## RMSE         0.96 ## ubRMSE       0.71 ## NRMSE %     52.30 ## PBIAS %     17.70 ## RSR          0.52 ## rSD          0.97 ## NSE          0.73 ## mNSE         0.54 ## rNSE         0.34 ## wNSE         0.89 ## wsNSE        0.76 ## d            0.93 ## dr           0.77 ## md           0.76 ## rd           0.83 ## cp          -1.17 ## r            0.92 ## R2           0.73 ## bR2          0.65 ## VE           0.82 ## KGE         -0.18 ## KGElf       -0.08 ## KGEnp        0.75 ## KGEkm        0.80 ## sKGE        -0.09 ## APFB         0.02 ## HFB          0.96 ## rSpearman    0.84 ## pbiasFDC % -32.96

what: A short example from hydrological modelling
title: Goodness-of-fit measures to compare observed and simulated time series with hydroGOF
text: Loading observed streamflows Ega River (Spain), daily data 1961-Jan-01 1970-Dec-31 Generating simulated daily time series, initially equal observed values (simulated values usually read output files hydrological model) Computing numeric goodness--fit measures “best” (unattainable) case Randomly changing first 1826 elements ‘sim’ (half ts), using normal distribution mean 10 standard deviation equal 1 (default ‘rnorm’). Plotting graphical comparison ‘obs’ ‘sim’, along numeric goodness--fit measures daily monthly time series
code: require(zoo) data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts sim <- obs gof(sim=sim, obs=obs) ##         [,1] ## ME         0 ## MAE        0 ## MSE        0 ## RMSE       0 ## ubRMSE     0 ## NRMSE %    0 ## PBIAS %    0 ## RSR        0 ## rSD        1 ## NSE        1 ## mNSE       1 ## rNSE       1 ## wNSE       1 ## wsNSE      1 ## d          1 ## dr         1 ## md         1 ## rd         1 ## cp         1 ## r          1 ## R2         1 ## bR2        1 ## VE         1 ## KGE        0 ## KGElf      0 ## KGEnp      1 ## KGEkm      1 ## sKGE       0 ## APFB       0 ## HFB        1 sim[1:1826] <- obs[1:1826] + rnorm(1826, mean=10) ggof(sim=sim, obs=obs, ftype="dm", FUN=mean)

title: Goodness-of-fit measures to compare observed and simulated time series with hydroGOF
text: Using first two years (1961-1962) warm-period, removing corresponding observed simulated values computation goodness--fit measures:  Verification goodness--fit measures daily values removing warm-period:
code: ggof(sim=sim, obs=obs, ftype="dm", FUN=mean, cal.ini="1963-01-01") sim <- window(sim, start="1963-01-01") obs <- window(obs, start="1963-01-01")  gof(sim, obs) ##          [,1] ## ME       3.75 ## MAE      3.75 ## MSE     37.84 ## RMSE     6.15 ## ubRMSE   4.88 ## NRMSE % 33.80 ## PBIAS % 25.20 ## RSR      0.34 ## rSD      1.02 ## NSE      0.89 ## mNSE     0.68 ## rNSE    -0.48 ## wNSE     0.98 ## wsNSE    0.82 ## d        0.97 ## dr       0.84 ## md       0.84 ## rd       0.64 ## cp       0.52 ## r        0.97 ## R2       0.89 ## bR2      0.81 ## VE       0.75 ## KGE     -0.25 ## KGElf   -0.06 ## KGEnp    0.69 ## KGEkm    0.73 ## sKGE    -0.30 ## APFB     0.03 ## HFB      1.00

text: Generating fictitious lower upper uncertainty bounds:  Plotting previously generated uncertainty bands:  Randomly generating simulated time series: Plotting previously generated simualted time series along obsertations uncertainty bounds:
code: lband <- obs - 5 uband <- obs + 5 plotbands(obs, lband, uband) plotbands(obs, lband, uband) sim <- obs + rnorm(length(obs), mean=3) plotbands(obs, lband, uband, sim)

code: r <- sim-obs library(hydroTSM) smry(r) ##               Index         r ## Min.     1963-01-01   -0.3042 ## 1st Qu.  1964-12-31    2.3620 ## Median   1966-12-31    3.0400 ## Mean     1966-12-31    3.0310 ## 3rd Qu.  1968-12-30    3.7110 ## Max.     1970-12-31    6.4810 ## IQR            <NA>    1.3488 ## sd             <NA>    1.0070 ## cv             <NA>    0.3323 ## Skewness       <NA>   -0.0700 ## Kurtosis       <NA>   -0.0253 ## NA's           <NA>    2.0000 ## n              <NA> 2922.0000 # daily, monthly and annual plots, boxplots and histograms hydroplot(r, FUN=mean) # daily, monthly and annual plots, boxplots and histograms hydroplot(r, FUN=mean, pfreq="seasonal")

title: Goodness-of-Fit Functions for Comparison of Simulated and Observed Hydrological Time Series
text: hydroGOF R package developed provide rigorous consistent framework evaluating performance hydrological environmental models. implements broad suite widely used statistical graphical goodness--fit metrics compare simulated values observed counterparts; coefficient determination (R²), Nash–Sutcliffe efficiency (NSE), Kling–Gupta efficiency (KGE), percent bias (PBIAS); support objective assessment model behaviour calibration, validation, operational application. package designed practical modelling workflows mind. functions facilitate transparent comparison observed simulated time series, enable systematic performance diagnostics, handle common data challenges missing values controlled reproducible manner. standardising computation performance indicators, hydroGOF helps ensure model evaluation remains methodologically consistent across studies applications. hydroGOF widely used research, teaching, professional practice, makes particularly suitable users require dependable, well-documented tools quantify model accuracy communicate results clarity. provides technically robust foundation evidence-based model development, benchmarking, decision support hydrology related environmental sciences.

title: Goodness-of-Fit Functions for Comparison of Simulated and Observed Hydrological Time Series
text: Quantitative statistics included package : : Mean Error (Hill et al., 2006) mae: Mean Absolute Error (Hodson, 2022) mse: Mean Squared Error (Yapo et al., 1996) rmse: Root Mean Square Error (Willmott Matsuura, 2005) ubRMSE: Unbiased Root Mean Square Error (Entekhabi et al., 2010) nrmse: Normalized Root Mean Square Error pbias: Percent Bias (Yapo et al., 1996) rsr: Ratio RMSE Standard Deviation Observations (Moriasi et al., 2007) rSD: Ratio Standard Deviations NSE: Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency (Nash Sutcliffe, 1970) mNSE: Modified Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency (Krause et al., 2005) rNSE: Relative Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency (Legates McCabe, 1999) wNSE: Weighted Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency (Hundecha Bardossy, 2004) wsNSE: Weighted Seasonal Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency (Zambrano-Bigiarini Bellin, ., 2012) d: Index Agreement (Willmott, C.J., 1981) dr: Refined Index Agreement (Willmott et al., 2012) md: Modified Index Agreement (Krause et al., 2005) rd: Relative Index Agreement (Krause et al., 2005) cp: Persistence Index (Kitanidis Bras, 1980) rPearson: Pearson correlation coefficient (Pearson, 1920) R2: Coefficient determination (Box, 1966) br2: R2 multiplied coefficient regression line   (Krause et al., 2005) VE: Volumetric efficiency (Criss Winston, 2008) KGE: Kling-Gupta efficiency (Gupta et al., 2009) KGElf: Kling-Gupta Efficiency low values (Garcia et al., 2017) KGEnp: Non-parametric version Kling-Gupta Efficiency (Pool et al., 2018) KGEkm: Knowable Moments Kling-Gupta Efficiency (Pizarro Jorquera, 2024) sKGE: Split Kling-Gupta Efficiency (Fowler et al., 2018) JDKGE: Joint Divergence Kling-Gupta Efficiency (Ficchi et al., 2026) APFB: Annual Peak Flow Bias (Mizukami et al., 2019) HFB: High Flow Bias PMR: Proxy Model Robustness (Royer-Gaspard et al., 2021) LME: Liu-Mean Efficiency (Liu, 2020) rSpearman: Spearman’s rank correlation coefficient (Spearman, 1961) ssq: Sum Squared Residuals (Willmott et al., 2009) pbiasfdc: PBIAS slope midsegment flow duration curve (Yilmaz et al., 2008) pfactor: P-factor (Abbaspour et al., 2009) rfactor: R-factor (Abbaspour et al., 2009)

title: Annual Peak Flow Bias — APFB
text: annual peak flow bias (APFB; Mizukami et al., 2019) designed drive calibration hydrological models focused reproduction high-flow events. computation index, annual peak flow first identified hydrological year sim obs. mean resulting annual peak flow series computed separately simulated observed data. annual peak flow bias defined absolute relative difference mean simulated annual peak flow mean observed annual peak flow. $$APFB = \sqrt{ \left( \frac{\mu_{peak\,Q_s}}{\mu_{peak\,Q_o}} - 1 \right)^2 }$$ : \mu_{peak\,Q_s}: mean simulated annual peak flow series \mu_{peak\,Q_o}: mean observed annual peak flow series APFB metric ranges 0 Inf. optimal value 0, indicating perfect agreement simulated observed annual peak flows. Essentially, closer 0, similar magnitude simulated observed annual peak flows. APFB focuses exclusively annual maxima, particularly suitable calibration tasks targeting flood estimation, extreme-flow simulation, infrastructure design applications. However, APFB evaluates annual maxima, assess overall hydrograph dynamics typically used combination complementary metrics (e.g., KGE NSE) broader performance evaluation required.

APFB.PerYear -) sim obs data.frame/matrix, output numeric, mean annual peak flow bias obtained individual years sim obs. -) sim obs data.frame/matrix, output data.frame,  mean annual peak flow bias obtained individual years sim obs.

title: Annual Peak Flow Bias — APFB
code: ################## # Example 1: Looking at the difference between 'NSE', 'wNSE', and 'APFB' # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Simulated daily time series, created equal to the observed values and then  # random noise is added only to high flows, i.e., those equal or higher than  # the quantile 0.9 of the observed values. sim      <- obs hQ.thr   <- quantile(obs, probs=0.9, na.rm=TRUE) hQ.index <- which(obs >= hQ.thr) hQ.n     <- length(hQ.index) sim[hQ.index] <- sim[hQ.index] + rnorm(hQ.n, mean=mean(sim[hQ.index], na.rm=TRUE))  # Traditional Nash-Sutcliffe eficiency NSE(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.002299849  # Weighted Nash-Sutcliffe efficiency (Hundecha and Bardossy, 2004) wNSE(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.2800836  # APFB (Mizukami et al., 2019): APFB(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.2941325  ################## # Example 2:  # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Generating a simulated daily time series, initially equal to the observed series sim <- obs   # Computing the 'APFB' for the "best" (unattainable) case APFB(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0  ################## # Example 3: APFB for simulated values created equal to the observed values and then  #            random noise is added only to high flows, i.e., those equal or higher  #            than the quantile 0.9 of the observed values.  sim           <- obs hQ.thr        <- quantile(obs, probs=0.9, na.rm=TRUE) hQ.index      <- which(obs >= hQ.thr) hQ.n          <- length(hQ.index) sim[hQ.index] <- sim[hQ.index] + rnorm(hQ.n, mean=mean(sim[hQ.index], na.rm=TRUE)) ggof(sim, obs)   APFB(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.293427  ################## # Example 4: APFB for simulated values created equal to the observed values and then  #            random noise is added only to high flows, i.e., those equal or higher   #            than the quantile 0.9 of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations.  APFB(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log) #> [1] 0.06809021  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim) lobs <- log(obs) APFB(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.06809021   ################## # Example 5: APFB for simulated values created equal to the observed values and then  #            random noise is added only to high flows, i.e., those equal or higher   #            than the quantile 0.9 of the observed values and applying a  #            user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  APFB(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1) #> [1] 0.1622292  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) APFB(sim=sim1, obs=obs1) #> [1] 0.1622292

title: Median annual high-flow bias — HFB
text: median annual high-flow bias (HFB) goodness--fit metric designed support calibration evaluation hydrological models specific emphasis reproduction high-flow conditions. HFB ranges 0 1, optimal value 1. Values close 1 indicate simulated high flows closely match observed high flows,  whereas values approaching 0 indicate increasing discrepancies simulated observed high-flow magnitudes. HFB function inspired annual peak-flow bias (APFB) objective function proposed Mizukami et al. (2019). However, differs metric four important aspects: 1) Instead considering single observed annual peak flow year, considers high flows year, "high flows" defined values equal greater user-defined exceedance probability threshold observed values. default, high flows correspond upper 10% observed flows (.e., hQ.thr=0.1). 2) Instead selecting simulated high flows independently observed events, evaluates simulated flows occurring time steps observed high flows, thereby preserving temporal correspondence observed simulated events. 3) year, metric uses median individual high-flow ratios rather single annual peak-flow value, providing robust summary high-flow bias within year. 4) computing final performance value, metric uses median annual values instead mean, reducing influence extreme years improving robustness distribution annual biases asymmetric. Mathematically, annual high-flow performance year \(y\) defined : $$ HFB_y = 1 - \left| \operatorname{median} \left( \frac{Q^{sim}_{y,}}{Q^{obs}_{y,}} \right) - 1 \right| $$ \(Q^{sim}_{y,}\) \(Q^{obs}_{y,}\) simulated observed flows corresponding set high-flow events \(\) occurring year \(y\). overall HFB value computed : $$ HFB = 1 - \left| \operatorname{median}(HFB_y) - 1 \right| $$ formulation ensures metric bounded 0 1, maximum value 1 representing perfect agreement simulated observed high flows.

code: ################## # Example 1: Looking at the difference between 'NSE', 'wNSE', and 'HFB' # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Simulated daily time series, created equal to the observed values and then  # random noise is added only to high flows, i.e., those equal or higher than  # the quantile 0.9 of the observed values. sim      <- obs hQ.thr   <- quantile(obs, probs=0.9, na.rm=TRUE) hQ.index <- which(obs >= hQ.thr) hQ.n     <- length(hQ.index) sim[hQ.index] <- sim[hQ.index] + rnorm(hQ.n, mean=mean(sim[hQ.index], na.rm=TRUE))  # Traditional Nash-Sutcliffe eficiency NSE(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.005488789  # Weighted Nash-Sutcliffe efficiency (Hundecha and Bardossy, 2004) wNSE(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.2825219  # HFB (Garcia et al., 2017): HFB(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] -0.1627644  ################## # Example 2:  # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Generating a simulated daily time series, initially equal to the observed series sim <- obs   # Computing the 'HFB' for the "best" (unattainable) case HFB(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 1  ################## # Example 3: HFB for simulated values created equal to the observed values and then  #            random noise is added only to high flows, i.e., those equal or higher than  # Example 4: HFB for simulated values created equal to the observed values and then  #            random noise is added only to high flows, i.e., those equal or higher than  #            the quantile 0.9 of the observed values and applying (natural)  # HFB(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log) #> [1] 0.9233967  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim) lobs <- log(obs) HFB(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.9233967   ################## # Example 5: HFB for simulated values created equal to the observed values and then  #            random noise is added only to high flows, i.e., those equal or higher than  #            the quantile 0.9 of the observed values and applying a  #            user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  HFB(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1) #> [1] 0.832271  # Verifying the previous value sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) HFB(sim=sim1, obs=obs1) #> [1] 0.832271

title: Joint Divergence Kling-Gupta Efficiency — JDKGE
text: Joint Divergence Kling-Gupta Efficiency sim obs, treatment missing values. implementation follows technical formulation described Ficchi et al. (2026): KGE' variability term used, distributional component computed histogram-based Jensen-Shannon divergence applied log-transformed flows paper-specific zero handling. function also allows JDKGE-style variants based 2009 2021 KGE formulations, keeping paper's 2012 variant default.

title: Joint Divergence Kling-Gupta Efficiency — JDKGE
code: JDKGE(sim, obs, ...)  # Default S3 method JDKGE(sim, obs, s=c(1,1,1,1), na.rm=TRUE,               method=c("2012", "2009", "2021"), out.type=c("single", "full"), fun=NULL, ...,               epsilon.type=c("paper", "none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),               epsilon.value=NA, density.method=c("hist", "kde", "wasserstein"), nbins="paper",               timestep=86400, kde.n.grid=512, wasserstein.n.quantiles=512)  # S3 method for class 'data.frame' JDKGE(sim, obs, s=c(1,1,1,1), na.rm=TRUE,               method=c("2012", "2009", "2021"), out.type=c("single", "full"), fun=NULL, ...,               epsilon.type=c("paper", "none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),               epsilon.value=NA, density.method=c("hist", "kde", "wasserstein"), nbins="paper",               timestep=86400, kde.n.grid=512, wasserstein.n.quantiles=512)  # S3 method for class 'matrix' JDKGE(sim, obs, s=c(1,1,1,1), na.rm=TRUE,               method=c("2012", "2009", "2021"), out.type=c("single", "full"), fun=NULL, ...,               epsilon.type=c("paper", "none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),               epsilon.value=NA, density.method=c("hist", "kde", "wasserstein"), nbins="paper",               timestep=86400, kde.n.grid=512, wasserstein.n.quantiles=512)  # S3 method for class 'zoo' JDKGE(sim, obs, s=c(1,1,1,1), na.rm=TRUE,               method=c("2012", "2009", "2021"), out.type=c("single", "full"), fun=NULL, ...,               epsilon.type=c("paper", "none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),               epsilon.value=NA, density.method=c("hist", "kde", "wasserstein"), nbins="paper",               timestep=NA, kde.n.grid=512, wasserstein.n.quantiles=512)

title: Joint Divergence Kling-Gupta Efficiency — JDKGE
text: sim numeric, zoo, matrix data.frame simulated values. obs numeric, zoo, matrix data.frame observed values. s numeric length 4 scaling factors Euclidean distance criteria space. s differs c(1,1,1,1), sum(s) must equal 1. na.rm logical value indicating whether missing paired values removed computing metric. method character string indicating Kling-Gupta formulation used JDKGE. Valid values "2012" (default, paper's KGE' formulation), "2009", "2021". .type character string indicating output format. Use "single" return JDKGE value , "full" also return four diagnostic components. fun optional function applied sim obs computing JDKGE. first argument fun must numeric vector. ... additional arguments passed fun. epsilon.type rule used zero-flow handling internal log-based divergence component, also passed preproc fun needs offset transformation. "paper" default uses epsilon rule described Ficchi et al. (2026). epsilon.value numeric value used epsilon.type="otherValue" epsilon.type="otherFactor". density.method method used compute divergence component. "hist" uses paper-faithful histogram-based Jensen-Shannon divergence, "kde" uses common-grid kernel density estimate followed Jensen-Shannon divergence, "wasserstein" uses Wasserstein-distance similarity log-flows. nbins binning rule used histogram divergence component. default "paper" uses procedure described Ficchi et al. (2026). argument ignored density.method="kde" density.method="wasserstein". timestep sampling time step seconds used paper's bin-count adjustment. zoo inputs inferred time index omitted. default plain numeric vectors one day (86400 seconds). kde.n.grid integer, number grid points used density.method="kde". Larger values provide finer common support grid higher computational cost. wasserstein.n.quantiles integer, number quantile levels used approximate first Wasserstein distance density.method="wasserstein". Larger values provide finer approximation higher computational cost.

title: Joint Divergence Kling-Gupta Efficiency — JDKGE
text: JDKGE combines four components: Pearson correlation coefficient \(r\), variability term, defined \(\gamma = (\sigma_s / \mu_s) / (\sigma_o / \mu_o)\) method="2012" \(\alpha = \sigma_s / \sigma_o\) method="2009" method="2021", bias term, defined \(\beta = \mu_s / \mu_o\) method="2009" method="2012", \(\beta_{2021} = (\mu_s - \mu_o)/\sigma_o\) method="2021", distributional similarity component \(\Delta\). divergence component, implementation follows paper's workflow: exact zeros replaced according epsilon.type. default "paper", \(\epsilon = \min(10^{-6}, 10^{-1} \min(c))\), \(c\) set strictly positive simulated observed values, transformed values \(\log(x)\) binned using histogram, Freedman-Diaconis width lower-bounded \(h_{min} = \min(10^2 \epsilon, 10^{-1})\), number bins adjusted time-scale factor clipped interval \([25, 100]\), additive smoothing \(\alpha = \epsilon\) applied empirical densities, Jensen-Shannon divergence computed base-2 logarithms. density.method="kde", simulated observed log-flows smoothed Gaussian kernel density estimates evaluated common grid pooled support using shared bandwidth estimated pooled sample. Jensen-Shannon divergence computed two resulting probability vectors. density.method="wasserstein", log-flow distributions compared first Wasserstein distance computed empirical quantiles. resulting distance converted similarity component $$\Delta = \exp(-W_1 / s_w)$$ \(s_w\) robust scale estimated pooled log-flows using interquartile range, fallback standard deviation needed. metric computed : $$JDKGE = 1 - \sqrt{(s[1](r-1))^2 + (s[2](vr-1))^2 + (s[3](br-1))^2 + (s[4](\Delta-1))^2}$$ Joint Divergence Kling-Gupta efficiencies range \(-\infty\) 1. Values closer 1 indicate stronger agreement simulated observed values across correlation, variability, bias, distributional similarity.

title: Joint Divergence Kling-Gupta Efficiency — JDKGE

JDKGE.elements numeric 4 elements: ‘r’, selected bias term, selected variability term, ‘Delta’.

title: Joint Divergence Kling-Gupta Efficiency — JDKGE
code: # Example 0.1: ideal case obs <- 1:10 sim <- 1:10  JDKGE(sim, obs) #> [1] 1  ################## # Example 1: simulated values equal to twice the observations sim <- 2*obs JDKGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, out.type="full") #> $JDKGE.value #> [1] -0.1180287 #>  #> $JDKGE.elements #>         r      Beta     Gamma     Delta  #> 1.0000000 2.0000000 1.0000000 0.5000119  #>   ################## # Example 2: using kernel density estimation, instead of histograms (the default) JDKGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, density.method="kde") #> [1] -0.005512615 JDKGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, density.method="kde", kde.n.grid=1024) #> [1] -0.005471278 JDKGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, density.method="wasserstein") #> [1] -0.1241596 JDKGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, density.method="wasserstein", wasserstein.n.quantiles=1024) #> [1] -0.1241596  ################## # Example 3: Looking at the difference between JDKGE and KGE, both with 'method=2009'  #            and 'method=2012' # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Simulated daily time series, initially equal to twice the observed values sim <- 2*obs   # KGE 2009 KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2009", out.type="full") #> $KGE.value #> [1] -1.236068 #>  #> $KGE.elements #>     r  Beta Alpha  #>     1     2     2  #>   # JDKGE (Ficchi et al., 2026): JDKGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2009", out.type="full") #> $JDKGE.value #> [1] -0.4243983 #>  #> $JDKGE.elements #>         r      Beta     Alpha     Delta  #> 1.0000000 2.0000000 2.0000000 0.8299693  #>   # KGE 2012 KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2012", out.type="full") #> $KGE.value #> [1] -1 #>  #> $KGE.elements #>     r  Beta Gamma  #>     1     2     1  #>   # JDKGE (Ficchi et al., 2026): JDKGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2012", out.type="full") #> $JDKGE.value #> [1] -0.01435222 #>  #> $JDKGE.elements #>         r      Beta     Gamma     Delta  #> 1.0000000 2.0000000 1.0000000 0.8299693  #>   # KGE 2021 KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2021", out.type="full") #> $KGE.value #> [1] -0.2744084 #>  #> $KGE.elements #>         r Beta.2021     Alpha  #> 1.0000000 0.7900105 2.0000000  #>   # JDKGE (Ficchi et al., 2026): JDKGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2021", out.type="full") #> $JDKGE.value #> [1] -0.03586003 #>  #> $JDKGE.elements #>         r Beta.2021     Alpha     Delta  #> 1.0000000 0.7900105 2.0000000 0.8299693  #>   ################## # Example 4:  # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Generating a simulated daily time series, initially equal to the observed series sim <- obs   # Computing the 'JDKGE' for the "best" (unattainable) case JDKGE(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 1  ################## # Example 5: JDKGE for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values.  #            This random noise has more relative importance for ow flows than  #            for medium and high flows.    # Randomly changing the first 1826 elements of 'sim', by using a normal distribution  # with mean 10 and standard deviation equal to 1 (default of 'rnorm'). sim[1:1826] <- obs[1:1826] + rnorm(1826, mean=10) ggof(sim, obs)   JDKGE(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.6083153  ################## # Example 6: JDKGE for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations.  JDKGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log) #> Error in match.arg(epsilon.type): 'arg' should be one of “none”, “Pushpalatha2012”, “otherFactor”, “otherValue”  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim) lobs <- log(obs) JDKGE(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.6262745  ################## # Example 7: JDKGE for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding the Pushpalatha2012 constant #            during computations  JDKGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="Pushpalatha2012") #> [1] 0.6393235  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 eps  <- mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE)/100 lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) JDKGE(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.6353161  ################## # Example 8: JDKGE for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding a user-defined constant #            during computations  eps <- 0.01 JDKGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherValue", epsilon.value=eps) #> [1] 0.6296324  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) JDKGE(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.626984  ################## # Example 9: JDKGE for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and using a user-defined factor #            to multiply the mean of the observed values to obtain the constant #            to be added to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fact <- 1/50 JDKGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherFactor", epsilon.value=fact) #> [1] 0.6490924  # Verifying the previous value: eps  <- fact*mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE) lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) JDKGE(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.6437007  ################## # Example 10: JDKGE for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #             on the first half of the observed values and applying a  #             user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  JDKGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1) #> Error in match.arg(epsilon.type): 'arg' should be one of “none”, “Pushpalatha2012”, “otherFactor”, “otherValue”  # Verifying the previous value sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) JDKGE(sim=sim1, obs=obs1) #> [1] 0.7286612  ################## # Example 11: JDKGE for a two-column data frame where simulated values are equal to  #             observations plus random noise on the first half of the observed values   SIM <- cbind(sim, sim) OBS <- cbind(obs, obs)  JDKGE(sim=SIM, obs=OBS) #>       obs       obs  #> 0.6083153 0.6083153

text: sim numeric, zoo, matrix data.frame simulated values obs numeric, zoo, matrix data.frame observed values s numeric length 3, representing scaling factors used re-scaling criteria space computing Euclidean distance ideal point c(1,1,1), .e., s elements used adjusting emphasis different components. first elements used rescaling Pearson product-moment correlation coefficient (r), second element used rescaling Alpha third element used re-scaling Beta na.rm logical value indicating whether 'NA' stripped computation proceeds.  'NA' value found -th position obs sim, -th value obs sim removed computation. method character, indicating formula used compute variability ratio Kling-Gupta efficiency. Valid values : -) 2009: variability defined ‘Alpha’, ratio standard deviation sim values standard deviation obs. default option. See Gupta et al. (2009). -) 2012: variability defined ‘Gamma’, ratio coefficient variation sim values coefficient variation obs. See Kling et al. (2012). -) 2021: bias defined ‘Beta’, ratio mean(sim) minus mean(obs) standard deviation obs. variability defined ‘Alpha’, ratio standard deviation sim values standard deviation obs. See Tang et al. (2021). .type character, indicating whether output function include one three terms used computation Kling-Gupta efficiency . Valid values : -) single: output numeric Kling-Gupta efficiency . -) full: output list two elements: first one Kling-Gupta efficiency, second numeric 3 elements: Pearson product-moment correlation coefficient (‘r’), ratio mean simulated values mean observations (‘Beta’), variability measure (‘Gamma’ ‘Alpha’, depending value method). fun function applied sim obs order obtain transformed values thereof computing Kling-Gupta efficiency. first argument MUST numeric vector name (e.g., x), additional arguments passed using .... ... arguments passed fun, addition mandatory first numeric vector. epsilon.type argument used define numeric value added sim obs applying fun.  designed allow use logarithm similar functions work zero values. Valid values epsilon.type : 1) "none": sim obs used fun without addition nummeric value. 2) "Pushpalatha2012": one hundredth (1/100) mean observed values added sim obs applying fun, described Pushpalatha et al. (2012). 3) "otherFactor": numeric value defined epsilon.value argument used multiply mean observed values, instead one hundredth (1/100) described Pushpalatha et al. title: Kling-Gupta Efficiency — KGE
text: computation index, three main components involved: 1) r    : Pearson product-moment correlation coefficient. Ideal value r=1. 2) Beta : ratio mean simulated values mean observed ones. Ideal value Beta=1. 3) vr   : variability ratio, computed using standard deviation (Alpha) coefficient variation (Gamma) sim obs, depending value method: 3.1) Alpha: ratio standard deviation simulated values standard deviation observed ones. ideal value Alpha=1. 3.2) Gamma: ratio coefficient variation (CV) simulated values coefficient variation observed ones. ideal value Gamma=1. full discussion Kling-Gupta index, advantages Nash-Sutcliffe efficiency (NSE) see Gupta et al. (2009). Kling-Gupta efficiencies range -Inf 1. Essentially, closer 1, similar sim obs . Knoben et al. (2019) showed KGE values greater -0.41 indicate model improves upon mean flow benchmark, even model's KGE value negative. $$KGE = 1 - ED$$ $$ ED =  \sqrt{ (s[1]*(r-1))^2 +(s[2]*(vr-1))^2 + (s[3]*(\beta-1))^2 } $$ $$r=Pearson product-moment correlation coefficient$$ $$vr=  \left\{   \begin{array}{cc}     \alpha & , \: method=2009 \\     \gamma & , \: method=2012   \end{array} \right.$$ $$\beta=\mu_s/\mu_o$$ $$\alpha=\sigma_s/\sigma_o$$ $$\gamma=\frac{CV_s}{CV_o} = \frac{\sigma_s/\mu_s}{\sigma_o/\mu_o}$$

code: # Example1: basic ideal case obs <- 1:10 sim <- 1:10 KGE(sim, obs) #> [1] 0  obs <- 1:10 sim <- 2:11 KGE(sim, obs) #> [1] -0.1818182  ################## # Example2: Looking at the difference between 'method=2009' and 'method=2012' # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Simulated daily time series, initially equal to twice the observed values sim <- 2*obs   # Traditional Kling-Gupta eficiency (Gupta and Kling, 2009) KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2009", out.type="full") #> $KGE.value #> [1] -1.236068 #>  #> $KGE.elements #>     r  Beta Alpha  #>     1     2     2  #>   # KGE': Kling-Gupta eficiency 2012 (Kling et al.,2012)  KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2012", out.type="full") #> $KGE.value #> [1] -1 #>  #> $KGE.elements #>     r  Beta Gamma  #>     1     2     1  #>   # KGE'': Kling-Gupta eficiency 2021 (Tang et al.,2021)  KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2021", out.type="full") #> $KGE.value #> [1] -0.2744084 #>  #> $KGE.elements #>         r Beta.2021     Alpha  #> 1.0000000 0.7900105 2.0000000  #>   ################## # Example3: KGE for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #           on the first half of the observed values # Randomly changing the first 1826 elements of 'sim', by using a normal distribution  # with mean 10 and standard deviation equal to 1 (default of 'rnorm'). sim <- obs  sim[1:1826] <- obs[1:1826] + rnorm(1826, mean=10)  # Computing the new 'KGE' KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] -0.3178195  # Randomly changing the first 2000 elements of 'sim', by using a normal distribution  # with mean 10 and standard deviation equal to 1 (default of 'rnorm'). sim[1:2000] <- obs[1:2000] + rnorm(2000, mean=10)  # Traditional Kling-Gupta eficiency (Gupta and Kling, 2009) KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2009", out.type="full") #> $KGE.value #> [1] -0.3465351 #>  #> $KGE.elements #>         r      Beta     Alpha  #> 0.9707048 1.3455522 1.0422804  #>   # KGE': Kling-Gupta eficiency 2012 (Kling et al.,2012)  KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2012", out.type="full") #> $KGE.value #> [1] -0.3646131 #>  #> $KGE.elements #>         r      Beta     Gamma  #> 0.9707048 1.3455522 0.7746116  #>   # KGE'': Kling-Gupta eficiency 2021 (Tang et al.,2021)  KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2021", out.type="full") #> $KGE.value #> [1] 0.7222063 #>  #> $KGE.elements #>         r Beta.2021     Alpha  #> 0.9707048 0.2729899 1.0422804  #>   ################## # Example 4: KGE for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations.  KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log) #> [1] -0.2137016  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim) lobs <- log(obs) KGE(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] -0.2137016  ################## # Example 5: KGE for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding the Pushpalatha2012 constant #            during computations  KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="Pushpalatha2012") #> [1] -0.207003  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 eps  <- mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE)/100 lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) KGE(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] -0.207003  ################## # Example 6: KGE for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding a user-defined constant #            during computations  eps <- 0.01 KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherValue", epsilon.value=eps) #> [1] -0.213262  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) KGE(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] -0.213262  ################## # Example 7: KGE for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and using a user-defined factor #            to multiply the mean of the observed values to obtain the constant #            to be added to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fact <- 1/50 KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherFactor", epsilon.value=fact) #> [1] -0.2007963  # Verifying the previous value: eps  <- fact*mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE) lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) KGE(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] -0.2007963  ################## # Example 8: KGE for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying a  #            user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1) #> [1] -0.1921613  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) KGE(sim=sim1, obs=obs1) #> [1] -0.1921613

title: Kling-Gupta Efficiency with knowable-moments — KGEkm
text: Traditional objective functions, Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency (NSE) Kling-Gupta Efficiency (KGE), often make assumptions data distribution sensitive outliers. Kling-Gupta Efficiency knowable-moments (KGEkm) goodness--fit measure developed Pizarro Jorquera (2024) provide reliable estimation effective description high-order statistics typical hydrological samples , therefore, reducing uncertainty estimation computation KGE. \(KGE_{km}\), dispersion quantified using knowable moments (computed ordered values samples ascending order) instead standard deviation, retaining decomposition correlation, variability, bias components. general formulation Kling–Gupta Efficiency knowable moments (\(KGE_{km}\)) : $$ KGE_{km} = 1 - \sqrt{ \left[ s_1 (r - 1) \right]^2 + \left[ s_2 (vr - 1) \right]^2 + \left[ s_3 (br - 1) \right]^2 } $$ \(r\) Pearson product–moment correlation coefficient simulated (\(Q^{sim}_t\)) observed (\(Q^{obs}_t\)) values, \(vr\) variability ratio, \(br\) bias ratio, \(s = (s_1, s_2, s_3)\) vector non-negative scaling factors control relative importance component. Dispersion computed second knowable moment. sample \(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\), second knowable moment defined : $$ K_2 = \frac{1}{n(n-1)} \sum_{=1}^{n} 2 (-1) x_{()} $$ \(x_{()}\) denotes ordered values sample ascending order. corresponding dispersion measure : $$ \sigma_{km} = \sqrt{ 2 K_2 } $$ variability ratio depends selected method: method = "2009", variability defined ratio knowable-moment dispersions: $$ vr = \alpha = \frac{\sigma^{sim}_{km}}{\sigma^{obs}_{km}} $$ method = "2012", variability defined ratio coefficients variation: $$ vr = \gamma = \frac{ \sigma^{sim}_{km} / \mu^{sim} }{ \sigma^{obs}_{km} / \mu^{obs} } $$ method = "2021", variability defined 2009 formulation: $$ vr = \alpha = \frac{\sigma^{sim}_{km}}{\sigma^{obs}_{km}} $$ bias component also depends selected method: method = "2009" method = "2012": $$ br = \beta = \frac{\mu^{sim}}{\mu^{obs}} $$ method = "2021": $$ br = \beta_{2021} = \frac{ \mu^{sim} - \mu^{obs} }{ \sigma^{obs}_{km} } $$ line traditional Kling-Gupta efficiency, (\(KGE_{km}\)) ranges -Inf 1. Essentially, closer 1, similar sim obs . KGE-type metrics, statistic integrates information correlation, variability, bias single performance measure allowing explicit control relative contribution component scaling factors \(s\).

title: Kling-Gupta Efficiency with knowable-moments — KGEkm
code: # Example1: basic ideal case obs <- 1:10 sim <- 1:10 KGEkm(sim, obs) #> [1] 1  obs <- 1:10 sim <- 2:11 KGEkm(sim, obs) #> [1] 0.793454  ################## # Example2: Looking at the difference between 'method=2009' and 'method=2012'  # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Simulated daily time series, initially equal to twice the observed values sim <- 2*obs   # KGEkm 2012 (method="2012" is the default option for KGEkm) KGEkm(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2012", out.type="full") #> $KGEkm.value #> [1] -0.04201077 #>  #> $KGEkm.elements #>         r      Beta     Gamma  #> 1.0000000 2.0000000 0.7071068  #>   # KGEkm 2009 KGEkm(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2009", out.type="full") #> $KGEkm.value #> [1] -0.0823922 #>  #> $KGEkm.elements #>        r     Beta    Alpha  #> 1.000000 2.000000 1.414214  #>    ################## # Example 2: Looking at the difference between 'KGEkm', KGE', 'NSE', 'wNSE',  #            'wsNSE' and 'APFB' for detecting differences in high flows  # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Simulated daily time series, created equal to the observed values and then  # random noise is added only to high flows, i.e., those equal or higher than  # the quantile 0.9 of the observed values. sim      <- obs hQ.thr   <- quantile(obs, probs=0.9, na.rm=TRUE) hQ.index <- which(obs >= hQ.thr) hQ.n     <- length(hQ.index) sim[hQ.index] <- sim[hQ.index] + rnorm(hQ.n, mean=mean(sim[hQ.index], na.rm=TRUE))  # KGEkm (Pizarro and Jorquera, 2024; method='2012') KGEkm(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.578109  # KGE': Kling-Gupta eficiency 2012 (Kling et al.,2012)  KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2012") #> [1] -0.4352039  # Traditional Kling-Gupta eficiency (Gupta and Kling, 2009) KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] -0.6329255  # KGE'': Kling-Gupta eficiency 2021 (Tang et al.,2021)  KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2021") #> [1] 0.1010568  # Traditional Nash-Sutcliffe eficiency (Nash and Sutcliffe, 1970) NSE(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.004794342  # Weighted Nash-Sutcliffe efficiency (Hundecha and Bardossy, 2004) wNSE(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.281951  # wsNSE (Zambrano-Bigiarini and Bellin, 2012): wsNSE(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] -0.2213234  # APFB (Mizukami et al., 2019): APFB(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.2918397   ################## # Example 4: Looking at the difference between 'KGE', 'NSE', 'wsNSE', #            'dr', 'rd', 'md', and 'KGElf' for detecting  #            differences in low flows  # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Simulated daily time series, created equal to the observed values and then  # random noise is added only to low flows, i.e., those equal or lower than  # the quantile 0.4 of the observed values. sim      <- obs lQ.thr   <- quantile(obs, probs=0.4, na.rm=TRUE) lQ.index <- which(obs <= lQ.thr) lQ.n     <- length(lQ.index) sim[lQ.index] <- sim[lQ.index] + rnorm(lQ.n, mean=mean(sim[lQ.index], na.rm=TRUE))  # KGEkm (Pizarro and Jorquera, 2024; method='2012') KGEkm(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.8774177  # KGE': Kling-Gupta eficiency 2012 (Kling et al.,2012)  KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2012") #> [1] -0.1042023  # Traditional Kling-Gupta eficiency (Gupta and Kling, 2009) KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] -0.09772871  # KGE'': Kling-Gupta eficiency 2021 (Tang et al.,2021)  KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2021") #> [1] 0.912638  # Traditional Nash-Sutcliffe eficiency (Nash and Sutcliffe, 1970) NSE(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.9844205  # Weighted seasonal Nash-Sutcliffe efficiency (Zambrano-Bigiarini and Bellin, 2012): wsNSE(sim=sim, obs=obs, lambda=0.05, j=1/2) #> [1] 0.6815896  # Refined Index of Agreement (Willmott et al., 2012): dr(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.9394451  # Relative Index of Agreement (Krause et al., 2005): rd(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.9077374  # Modified Index of Agreement (Krause et al., 2005): md(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.9355419  # KGElf (Garcia et al., 2017): KGElf(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.00988583   ################## # Example 5: KGEkm for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations.  KGEkm(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log) #> [1] 0.8341867  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim) lobs <- log(obs) KGEkm(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.8341867  ################## # Example 6: KGEkm for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding the Pushpalatha2012 constant #            during computations  KGEkm(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="Pushpalatha2012") #> [1] 0.8408504  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 eps  <- mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE)/100 lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) KGEkm(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.8408504  ################## # Example 7: KGEkm for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding a user-defined constant #            during computations  eps <- 0.01 KGEkm(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherValue", epsilon.value=eps) #> [1] 0.8346255  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) KGEkm(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.8346255  ################## # Example 8: KGEkm for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and using a user-defined factor #            to multiply the mean of the observed values to obtain the constant #            to be added to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fact <- 1/50 KGEkm(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherFactor", epsilon.value=fact) #> [1] 0.8469799  # Verifying the previous value: eps  <- fact*mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE) lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) KGEkm(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.8469799  ################## # Example 9: KGEkm for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying a  #            user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  KGEkm(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1) #> [1] 0.8960538  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) KGEkm(sim=sim1, obs=obs1) #> [1] 0.8960538

title: Kling-Gupta Efficiency for low values — KGElf
text: Garcia et al. (2017) tested different objective functions found mean value KGE applied streamflows (.e., KGE(Q)) KGE applied inverse streamflows (.e., KGE(1/Q) able provide aceptable representation low-flow indices important water management. also found KGE applied transformation streamflow values (e.g., log)  inadequate capture low-flow indices important water management. robustness findings depends climate variability rather objective function, insensitive hydrological model used evaluation. $$KGE_{lf} =  \frac{KGE(Q) + KGE(1/Q)}{2} $$ Traditional Kling-Gupta efficiencies (Gupta et al., 2009; Kling et al., 2012) range -Inf 1 , therefore, KGElf also range -Inf 1. Essentially, closer 1, similar sim obs . Knoben et al. (2019) showed traditional Kling-Gupta (Gupta et al., 2009; Kling et al., 2012) values greater -0.41 indicate model improves upon mean flow benchmark, even model's KGE value negative.

code: ################## # Example1: basic ideal case obs <- 1:10 sim <- 1:10 KGElf(sim, obs) #> [1] 0  obs <- 1:10 sim <- 2:11 KGElf(sim, obs) #> [1] -0.02703023  ################## # Example2: Looking at the difference between 'method=2009' and 'method=2012' # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Simulated daily time series, initially equal to twice the observed values sim <- 2*obs   # KGE 2009 KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2009", out.type="full") #> $KGE.value #> [1] -1.236068 #>  #> $KGE.elements #>     r  Beta Alpha  #>     1     2     2  #>   # KGE 2012 KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2012", out.type="full") #> $KGE.value #> [1] -1 #>  #> $KGE.elements #>     r  Beta Gamma  #>     1     2     1  #>   # KGElf (Garcia et al., 2017): KGElf(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2012") #> [1] -0.2550394  ################## # Example3: KGElf for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #           on the first half of the observed values.  #           This random noise has more relative importance for low flows than  #           for medium and high flows.    # Randomly changing the first 1826 elements of 'sim', by using a normal distribution  # with mean 10 and standard deviation equal to 1 (default of 'rnorm'). sim <- obs  sim[1:1826] <- obs[1:1826] + rnorm(1826, mean=10) ggof(sim, obs)   # Computing 'KGElf' KGElf(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] -0.07520619  ################## # Example 4: KGElf for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations.  KGElf(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log) #> [1] -0.07520619  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim) lobs <- log(obs) KGElf(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] -0.1676462  ################## # Example 5: KGElf for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding the Pushpalatha2012 constant #            during computations  KGElf(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="Pushpalatha2012") #> [1] -0.07520619  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 eps  <- mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE)/100 lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) KGElf(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] -0.1195413  ################## # Example 6: KGElf for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding a user-defined constant #            during computations  eps <- 0.01 KGElf(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherValue", epsilon.value=eps) #> [1] -0.07955099  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) KGElf(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] -0.162855  ################## # Example 7: KGElf for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and using a user-defined factor #            to multiply the mean of the observed values to obtain the constant #            to be added to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fact <- 1/50 KGElf(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherFactor", epsilon.value=fact) #> [1] -0.07162864  # Verifying the previous value: eps  <- fact*mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE) lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) KGElf(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] -0.0964785  ################## # Example 8: KGElf for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying a  #            user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  KGElf(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1) #> [1] -0.07520619  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) KGElf(sim=sim1, obs=obs1) #> [1] -0.02961372

title: Non-parametric version of the Kling-Gupta Efficiency — KGEnp
text: non-paramettric verison Kling-Gupta efficiency keeps bias term Alpha (mean(sim) / mean(obs)), correlation uses Spearman rank coefficient instead Pearson product-moment coefficient; variability uses normalized flow-duration curve instead standard deviation (coefficient variation). proposed non-parametric based multi-objective function can seen useful alternative existing performance measures aiming acceptable simulations multiple hydrograph aspects (Pool et al., 2018). $$KGE_{np} = 1 - ED$$ $$ ED =  \sqrt{ ((\rho-1)^2 + (\alpha-1)^2 + (\beta-1)^2 } $$ $$\rho = \textrm{Spearman rank correlation coefficient}$$ $$\alpha = 1 - 0.5*sum( sim((k)) / (n*\mu_s) - obs(J(k)) / (n*\mu_o) )$$ $$\beta = \mu_s/\mu_o$$ Traditional Kling-Gupta efficiencies (Gupta et al., 2009; Kling et al., 2012) range -Inf 1, therefore KGEnp . Essentially, closer 1, similar sim obs . Knoben et al. (2019) showed traditional Kling-Gupta (Gupta et al., 2009; Kling et al., 2012) values greater -0.41 indicate model improves upon mean flow benchmark, even model's KGE value negative.

KGEnp(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log) #> [1] 0.7452  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim) lobs <- log(obs) KGEnp(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.7452  ################## # Example 5: KGEnp for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding the Pushpalatha2012 constant #            during computations  KGEnp(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="Pushpalatha2012") #> [1] 0.7452  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 eps  <- mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE)/100 lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) KGEnp(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.7503355  ################## # Example 6: KGEnp for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding a user-defined constant #            during computations  eps <- 0.01 KGEnp(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherValue", epsilon.value=eps) #> [1] 0.7452  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) KGEnp(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.7455385  ################## # Example 7: KGEnp for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and using a user-defined factor #            to multiply the mean of the observed values to obtain the constant #            to be added to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fact <- 1/50 KGEnp(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherFactor", epsilon.value=fact) #> [1] 0.7452  # Verifying the previous value: eps  <- fact*mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE) lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) KGEnp(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.7550453  ################## # Example 8: KGEnp for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying a  #            user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  KGEnp(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1) #> [1] 0.7533573  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) KGEnp(sim=sim1, obs=obs1) #> [1] 0.7533573

title: Lee and Choi Efficiency — LCE
text: Lee Choi Efficiency sim obs, treatment missing values. goodness--fit measure proposed Lee Choi (2022) alternative Liu-Mean Efficiency (LME), designed provide multi-dimensional diagnostically balanced evaluation model performance; jointly considering correlation, variability bias; whereas LME fundamentally single-error-based metric. Unlike single-error-based criteria, LCE explicitly combines correlation coefficient variability ratio two complementary terms, r*Alpha r/Alpha, order penalize inconsistent representations timing variability. short description components numeric range values, please see Details.

title: Lee and Choi Efficiency — LCE
text: computation index, three main diagnostic components involved: 1) r : Pearson product-moment correlation coefficient. Ideal value r=1. 2) Alpha : variability ratio, defined ratio standard deviation simulated values standard deviation observed ones. Ideal value Alpha=1. 3) Beta : bias ratio, defined ratio mean simulated values mean observed ones. Ideal value Beta=1. Lee Choi Efficiency combines terms two additional correlation-variability components: $$r\alpha$$ $$r/\alpha$$ terms ideal value 1. first term, \(r\alpha\), penalizes cases combined effect correlation variability inconsistent observed dynamics. second term, \(r/\alpha\), penalizes opposite imbalance correlation variability. Together, two terms make LCE sensitive compensating errors timing dispersion. Lee Choi efficiencies range -Inf 1. Essentially, closer 1, similar sim obs terms timing, variability bias. $$LCE = 1 - ED$$ $$ ED = \sqrt{ (r\alpha - 1)^2 + (r/\alpha - 1)^2 + (\beta - 1)^2 } $$ : $$r=Pearson product-moment correlation coefficient$$ $$\alpha=\sigma_s/\sigma_o$$ $$\beta=\mu_s/\mu_o$$ $$r\alpha=r\times\alpha$$ $$r/\alpha=r/\alpha$$ \(\mu_s\) \(\mu_o\) mean simulated observed values, respectively, \(\sigma_s\) \(\sigma_o\) corresponding standard deviations. value LCE=1 indicates perfect agreement simulated observed values. Values close 1 indicate strong agreement across correlation, variability bias, whereas negative values indicate progressively poorer model performance.

title: Liu-Mean Efficiency — LME
text: Liu-Mean Efficiency sim obs, treatment missing values. goodness--fit measure proposed Liu et al. (2020) alternative Nash-Sutcliffe efficiency (NSE), designed provide balanced assessment model performance normalising mean squared error using mean observed values instead variance. Liu-Mean Efficiency evaluates large error compared average level observations, making particularly useful hydrological applications mean value meaningful scale evaluating prediction accuracy. normalisation makes performance measure behave like dimensionless relative error, scaled characteristic magnitude variable. result, absolute error judged differently depending whether mean flow small large. short description metric numeric range values, please see Details.

title: Liu-Mean Efficiency — LME
text: Liu-Mean Efficiency (LME) based mean squared error (MSE) normalized squared mean observed values. formulation conceptually similar Nash-Sutcliffe efficiency, uses mean observations reference scaling factor instead variance. modification reduces sensitivity metric high variability makes performance evaluation directly interpretable terms proportional error relative mean magnitude observed variable. Liu-Mean Efficiency ranges -Inf 1. value : - LME = 1 indicates perfect agreement sim obs. - LME = 0 indicates mean squared error equals squared mean observed values. - LME < 0 indicates model predictions worse squared mean observed values. Essentially, closer LME value 1, similar sim obs . $$LME = 1 - \frac{MSE}{\mu_o^2}$$ $$MSE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{=1}^{n} (sim_i - obs_i)^2$$ $$\mu_o = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{=1}^{n} obs_i$$ : n number paired observations sim_i simulated value time step obs_i observed value time step \mu_o mean observed values

           and 'method=2012'  # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Simulated daily time series, initially equal to twice the observed values sim <- 2*obs   # KGE 2009 KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2009", out.type="full") #> $KGE.value #> [1] -1.236068 #>  #> $KGE.elements #>     r  Beta Alpha  #>     1     2     2  #>   # KGE 2012 KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2012", out.type="full") #> $KGE.value #> [1] -1 #>  #> $KGE.elements #>     r  Beta Gamma  #>     1     2     1  #>   # LME (Liu et al., 2020): LME(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2012") #> [1] -0.4142136  ################## # Example 2:  # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Generating a simulated daily time series, initially equal to the observed series sim <- obs   # Computing the 'LME' for the "best" (unattainable) case LME(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 1  ################## # Example 3: LME for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values.  #            This random noise has more relative importance for ow flows than  #            for medium and high flows.    # Randomly changing the first 1826 elements of 'sim', by using a normal distribution  # with mean 10 and standard deviation equal to 1 (default of 'rnorm'). sim[1:1826] <- obs[1:1826] + rnorm(1826, mean=10) ggof(sim, obs)   LME(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.6830412  ################## # Example 4: LME for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations.  LME(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log) #> [1] 0.6666261  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim) lobs <- log(obs) LME(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.6666261  ################## # Example 5: LME for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding the Pushpalatha2012 constant #            during computations  LME(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="Pushpalatha2012") #> [1] 0.6748451  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 eps  <- mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE)/100 lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) LME(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.6748451  ################## # Example 6: LME for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding a user-defined constant #            during computations  eps <- 0.01 LME(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherValue", epsilon.value=eps) #> [1] 0.6671667  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) LME(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.6671667  ################## # Example 7: LME for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and using a user-defined factor #            to multiply the mean of the observed values to obtain the constant #            to be added to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fact <- 1/50 LME(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherFactor", epsilon.value=fact) #> [1] 0.6824302  # Verifying the previous value: eps  <- fact*mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE) lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) LME(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.6824302  ################## # Example 8: LME for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying a  #            user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  LME(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1) #> [1] 0.7952498  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) LME(sim=sim1, obs=obs1) #> [1] 0.7952498  ################## # Example 9: LME for a two-column data frame where simulated values are equal to  #            observations plus random noise on the first half of the observed values   SIM <- cbind(sim, sim) OBS <- cbind(obs, obs)  LME(sim=SIM, obs=OBS) #>       obs       obs  #> 0.6830412 0.6830412   ################## # Example 10: LME for each year, where simulated values are given in a two-column data  #             frame equal to the observations plus random noise on the first half of the  #             observed values  SIM <- cbind(sim, sim) OBS <- cbind(obs, obs) LME(sim=SIM, obs=OBS, out.PerYear=TRUE) #>       obs       obs  #> 0.6830412 0.6830412

title: Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency — NSE
text: $$ NSE = 1 -\frac { \sum_{=1}^N { \left( S_i - O_i \right)^2 } } { \sum_{=1}^N { \left( O_i - \bar{O} \right)^2 } }  $$ Nash-Sutcliffe efficiency (NSE) normalized statistic determines relative magnitude residual variance ("noise") compared measured data variance ("information") (Nash Sutcliffe, 1970). NSE indicates well plot observed versus simulated data fits 1:1 line. Nash-Sutcliffe efficiencies range -Inf 1. Essentially, closer 1, accurate model .  -) NSE = 1, corresponds perfect match modelled observed data.  -) NSE = 0, indicates model predictions accurate mean observed data,  -) -Inf < NSE < 0, indicates observed mean better predictor model.

title: Proxy for Model Robustness — PMR
text: $$ PMR = 2 \times \frac{1}{N} \sum_{=1}^{N} \left| (\bar{S}_i - \bar{O}_i) - (\bar{S} - \bar{O}) \right| \frac{1}{\bar{O}} $$ : \(\bar{S}_i\) \(\bar{O}_i\) mean simulated observed values computed -th moving window length k, \(\bar{S}\) \(\bar{O}\) overall mean simulated observed values, \(N\) number moving windows. proxy model robustness (PMR) dimensionless statistic quantifies temporal stability model bias measuring average deviation sub-period bias overall bias. PMR indicates consistent model performance across different time periods hydrological conditions. proxy model robustness ranges 0 positive infinity. Essentially, closer 0, temporally robust model . -) PMR = 0 corresponds perfectly robust model, model bias identical across sub-periods. -) 0 < PMR < 1 indicates relatively stable model performance moderate temporal variability bias. -) PMR > 1 indicates increasing variability model bias across time periods, suggesting reduced robustness model performance.
