code: ################## # Example 1: Looking at the difference between PMR and KGE, both with 'method=2009'  #            and 'method=2012'  # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Simulated daily time series, initially equal to twice the observed values sim <- 2*obs   # KGE 2009 KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2009", out.type="full") #> $KGE.value #> [1] -1.236068 #>  #> $KGE.elements #>     r  Beta Alpha  #>     1     2     2  #>   # KGE 2012 KGE(sim=sim, obs=obs, method="2012", out.type="full") #> $KGE.value #> [1] -1 #>  #> $KGE.elements #>     r  Beta Gamma  #>     1     2     1  #>   # PMR (Royer-Gaspard et al., 2021): PMR(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.05855559  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ################## # Example 2:  # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Generating a simulated daily time series, initially equal to the observed series sim <- obs   # Computing the 'PMR' for the "best" (unattainable) case PMR(sim=sim, obs=obs)  ################## # Example 3: PMR for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values.  #            This random noise has more relative importance for low flows than  #            for medium and high flows.    # Randomly changing the first 1826 elements of 'sim', by using a normal distribution  # with mean 10 and standard deviation equal to 1 (default of 'rnorm'). sim[1:1826] <- obs[1:1826] + rnorm(1826, mean=10) ggof(sim, obs)  PMR(sim=sim, obs=obs)  ################## # Example 4: PMR for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations.  PMR(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log)  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim) lobs <- log(obs) PMR(sim=lsim, obs=lobs)  ################## # Example 5: PMR for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding the Pushpalatha2012 constant #            during computations  PMR(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="Pushpalatha2012")  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 eps  <- mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE)/100 lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) PMR(sim=lsim, obs=lobs)   ################## # Example 6: PMR for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding a user-defined constant #            during computations  eps <- 0.01 PMR(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherValue", epsilon.value=eps)  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) PMR(sim=lsim, obs=lobs)  ################## # Example 7: PMR for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and using a user-defined factor #            to multiply the mean of the observed values to obtain the constant #            to be added to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fact <- 1/50 PMR(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherFactor", epsilon.value=fact)  # Verifying the previous value: eps  <- fact*mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE) lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) PMR(sim=lsim, obs=lobs)  ################## # Example 8: PMR for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying a  #            user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  PMR(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1)  # Verifying the previous value sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) PMR(sim=sim1, obs=obs1)  ################## # Example 9: PMR for a two-column data frame where simulated values are equal to  #            observations plus random noise on the first half of the observed values   SIM <- cbind(sim, sim) OBS <- cbind(obs, obs)  PMR(sim=SIM, obs=OBS) } #            during computations  eps <- 0.01 R2(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherValue", epsilon.value=eps) #> [1] 0.478967  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) R2(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.478967  ################## # Example 7: R2 for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and using a user-defined factor #            to multiply the mean of the observed values to obtain the constant #            to be added to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fact <- 1/50 R2(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherFactor", epsilon.value=fact) #> [1] 0.4923961  # Verifying the previous value: eps  <- fact*mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE) lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) R2(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.4923961  ################## # Example 8: R2 for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying a  #            user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  R2(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1) #> [1] 0.7259939  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) R2(sim=sim1, obs=obs1) #> [1] 0.7259939

title: Coefficient of persistence — cp
text: $$ cp = 1 -\frac { \sum_{=2}^N { \left( S_i - O_i \right)^2 } } { \sum_{=1}^{N-1} { \left( O_{+1} - O_i \right)^2 } }  $$ Coefficient persistence (Kitadinis Bras, 1980; Corradini et al., 1986) used compare model  performance simple model using observed value previous day prediction current day. coefficient persistence compare predictions model predictions obtained assuming process Wiener process (variance increasing linearly time), case, best estimate future given latest measurement (Kitadinis Bras, 1980). Persistence model efficiency normalized model evaluation statistic quantifies relative magnitude residual variance (noise) variance errors obtained use simple persistence model (Moriasi et al., 2007). CP ranges 0 1, CP = 1 optimal value larger 0.0 indicate minimally acceptable model performance.

title: Index of Agreement — d
text: $$d = 1 - \frac{\sum_{=1}^N {(O_i - S_i)^2} } { \sum_{=1}^N { ( \left| S_i -  \bar{O} \right| + \left| O_i -  \bar{O}  \right| } )^2 } $$ Index Agreement (d) developed Willmott (1981) standardized measure degree model prediction error. dimensionless varies 0 1. value 1 indicates perfect match, 0 indicates agreement (Willmott, 1981). index agreement can detect additive proportional differences observed simulated means variances; however, overly sensitive extreme values due squared differences (Legates McCabe, 1999).

title: Index of Agreement — d
code: ################## # Example 1: basic ideal case obs <- 1:10 sim <- 1:10 d(sim, obs) #> [1] 1  obs <- 1:10 sim <- 2:11 d(sim, obs) #> [1] 0.9706745  ################## # Example 2:  # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Generating a simulated daily time series, initially equal to the observed series sim <- obs   # Computing the 'd' for the "best" (unattainable) case d(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 1  ################## # Example 3: d for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values.  #            This random noise has more relative importance for ow flows than  #            for medium and high flows.    # Randomly changing the first 1826 elements of 'sim', by using a normal distribution  # with mean 10 and standard deviation equal to 1 (default of 'rnorm'). sim[1:1826] <- obs[1:1826] + rnorm(1826, mean=10) ggof(sim, obs)   d(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.9698277  ################## # Example 4: d for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations.  d(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log) #> [1] 0.8616344  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim) lobs <- log(obs) d(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.8616344  ################## # Example 5: d for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding the Pushpalatha2012 constant #            during computations  d(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="Pushpalatha2012") #> [1] 0.8639616  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 eps  <- mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE)/100 lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) d(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.8639616  ################## # Example 6: d for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding a user-defined constant #            during computations  eps <- 0.01 d(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherValue", epsilon.value=eps) #> [1] 0.8617877  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) d(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.8617877  ################## # Example 7: d for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and using a user-defined factor #            to multiply the mean of the observed values to obtain the constant #            to be added to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fact <- 1/50 d(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherFactor", epsilon.value=fact) #> [1] 0.8661051  # Verifying the previous value: eps  <- fact*mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE) lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) d(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.8661051  ################## # Example 8: d for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying a  #            user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  d(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1) #> [1] 0.931498  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) d(sim=sim1, obs=obs1) #> [1] 0.931498

title: Refined Index of Agreement — dr
text: sim numeric, zoo, matrix data.frame simulated values obs numeric, zoo, matrix data.frame observed values na.rm logical value indicating whether 'NA' stripped computation proceeds.  'NA' value found -th position obs sim, -th value obs sim removed computation. fun function applied sim obs order obtain transformed values thereof computing Nash-Sutcliffe efficiency. first argument MUST numeric vector name (e.g., x), additional arguments passed using .... ... arguments passed fun, addition mandatory first numeric vector. epsilon.type argument used define numeric value added sim obs applying FUN.  designed allow use logarithm similar functions work zero values. Valid values epsilon.type : 1) "none": sim obs used FUN without addition nummeric value. 2) "Pushpalatha2012": one hundredth (1/100) mean observed values added sim obs applying FUN, described Pushpalatha et al. (2012). 3) "otherFactor": numeric value defined epsilon.value argument used multiply mean observed values, instead one hundredth (1/100) described Pushpalatha et al. (2012). resulting value added sim obs, applying FUN. 4) "otherValue": numeric value defined epsilon.value argument directly added sim obs, applying FUN. epsilon.value -) epsilon.type="otherValue" represents numeric value added sim obs applying fun.   -) epsilon.type="otherFactor" represents numeric factor used multiply mean observed values, instead one hundredth (1/100) described Pushpalatha et al. (2012). resulting value added sim obs applying fun.

title: Refined Index of Agreement — dr
text: $$c = 2$$ $$= \sum_{=1}^N {\left| S_i - O_i \right|} $$ $$B = c \sum_{=1}^N {\left| O_i - \bar{O} \right|} $$ $$dr = 1 - \frac{} { B } ; \leq B$$ $$dr = 1 - \frac{B} { } ; > B$$ Refined Index Agreement (dr, Willmott et al., 2012) reformulation orginal Willmott's index agreement developed 1980s (Willmott, 1981; Willmott, 1984; Willmott et al., 1985) Refined Index Agreement (dr) dimensionless, varies -1 1 (contrast original d, varies [0, 1]). Refined Index Agreement (dr) monotonically related modified Nash-Sutcliffe (E1) desribed Legates McCabe (1999). general, dr rationally related model accuracy existing indices (Willmott et al., 2012; Willmott et al., 2015). also quite flexible, making applicable wide range model-performance problems (Willmott et al., 2012)

title: Graphical Goodness of Fit — ggof
text: output gof function matrix one column , following rows: Mean Error MAE Mean Absolute Error MSE Mean Squared Error RMSE Root Mean Square Error ubRMSE Unbiased Root Mean Square Error NRMSE Normalized Root Mean Square Error  ( -100% <= NRMSE <= 100% ) PBIAS Percent Bias ( -Inf <= PBIAS <= Inf [%] ) RSR Ratio RMSE Standard Deviation Observations, RSR = rms / sd(obs). ( 0 <= RSR <= +Inf ) rSD Ratio Standard Deviations, rSD = sd(sim) / sd(obs) NSE Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency ( -Inf <= NSE <= 1 ) mNSE Modified Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency ( -Inf <= mNSE <= 1 ) rNSE Relative Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency ( -Inf <= rNSE <= 1 ) wNSE Weighted Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency ( -Inf <= wNSE <= 1 ) wsNSE Weighted Seasonal Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency ( -Inf <= wsNSE <= 1 ) d Index Agreement ( 0 <= d <= 1 ) dr Refined Index Agreement ( -1 <= dr <= 1 ) md Modified Index Agreement ( 0 <= md <= 1 ) rd Relative Index Agreement ( 0 <= rd <= 1 ) cp Persistence Index ( 0 <= cp <= 1 ) r Pearson Correlation coefficient ( -1 <= r <= 1 ) R2 Coefficient Determination ( 0 <= R2 <= 1 ) bR2 R2 multiplied coefficient regression line sim obs  ( 0 <= bR2 <= 1 ) VE Volumetric efficiency sim obs  ( -Inf <= VE <= 1) KGE Kling-Gupta efficiency sim obs  ( -Inf <= KGE <= 1 ) KGElf Kling-Gupta Efficiency low values sim obs  ( -Inf <= KGElf <= 1 ) KGEnp Non-parametric version Kling-Gupta Efficiency sim obs  ( -Inf <= KGEnp <= 1 ) KGEkm Knowable Moments Kling-Gupta Efficiency sim obs  ( -Inf <= KGEnp <= 1 ) following outputs produced sim obs zoo objects: sKGE Split Kling-Gupta Efficiency sim obs  ( -Inf <= sKGE <= 1 ). computed sim obs zoo objects APFB Annual Peak Flow Bias ( 0 <= APFB <= Inf ) HBF High Flow Bias ( 0 <= HFB <= Inf ) r.Spearman Spearman Correlation coefficient ( -1 <= r.Spearman <= 1 ). computed .spearman=TRUE pbiasfdc PBIAS slope midsegment Flow Duration Curve

title: Graphical Goodness of Fit — ggof
text: Mauricio Zambrano Bigiarini <mzb.devel@gmail.com>

title: Graphical Goodness of Fit — ggof
code: obs <- 1:10 sim <- 2:11  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ggof(sim, obs) } # }  ################## # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Generating a simulated daily time series, initially equal to the observed series sim <- obs   # Getting the numeric goodness of fit for the "best" (unattainable) case gof(sim=sim, obs=obs) #>         [,1] #> ME         0 #> MAE        0 #> MSE        0 #> RMSE       0 #> ubRMSE     0 #> NRMSE %    0 #> PBIAS %    0 #> RSR        0 #> rSD        1 #> NSE        1 #> mNSE       1 #> rNSE       1 #> wNSE       1 #> wsNSE      1 #> d          1 #> dr         1 #> md         1 #> rd         1 #> cp         1 #> r          1 #> R2         1 #> bR2        1 #> VE         1 #> KGE        0 #> KGElf      0 #> KGEnp      1 #> KGEkm      1 #> sKGE       0 #> APFB       0 #> HFB        1  # Randomly changing the first 2000 elements of 'sim', by using a normal distribution  # with mean 10 and standard deviation equal to 1 (default of 'rnorm'). sim[1:2000] <- obs[1:2000] + rnorm(2000, mean=10)  # Getting the new numeric goodness-of-fit measures gof(sim=sim, obs=obs) #>          [,1] #> ME       5.47 #> MAE      5.47 #> MSE     55.21 #> RMSE     7.43 #> ubRMSE   5.03 #> NRMSE % 37.10 #> PBIAS % 34.60 #> RSR      0.37 #> rSD      1.04 #> NSE      0.86 #> mNSE     0.57 #> rNSE    -0.57 #> wNSE     0.97 #> wsNSE    0.72 #> d        0.97 #> dr       0.78 #> md       0.78 #> rd       0.63 #> cp       0.42 #> r        0.97 #> R2       0.86 #> bR2      0.76 #> VE       0.65 #> KGE     -0.35 #> KGElf   -0.09 #> KGEnp    0.61 #> KGEkm    0.63 #> sKGE    -0.37 #> APFB     0.03 #> HFB      0.92  # Getting the graphical representation of 'obs' and 'sim' along with the numeric  # goodness-of-fit measures for the daily and monthly time series  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ggof(sim=sim, obs=obs, ftype="dm", FUN=mean) } # }  # Getting the graphical representation of 'obs' and 'sim' along with some numeric  # goodness-of-fit measures for the seasonal time series  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ggof(sim=sim, obs=obs, ftype="seasonal", FUN=mean) } # }  # Computing the daily residuals  # even if this is a dummy example, it is enough for illustrating the capability r <- sim-obs  # Summarizing and plotting the residuals if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(hydroTSM)  # summary smry(r)   # daily, monthly and annual plots, boxplots and histograms hydroplot(r, FUN=mean)  # seasonal plots and boxplots hydroplot(r, FUN=mean, pfreq="seasonal") } # }

title: Numerical Goodness-of-fit measures — gof
code: gof(sim, obs, ...)  # Default S3 method gof(sim, obs, na.rm=TRUE, do.spearman=FALSE, do.pbfdc=FALSE,         j=1, lambda=0.95, norm="sd", s=c(1,1,1), method=c("2009", "2012", "2021"),          lQ.thr=0.6, hQ.thr=0.1, start.month=1, digits=2, fun=NULL, ...,         epsilon.type=c("none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),          epsilon.value=NA)  # S3 method for class 'matrix' gof(sim, obs, na.rm=TRUE, do.spearman=FALSE, do.pbfdc=FALSE,         j=1, lambda=0.95, norm="sd", s=c(1,1,1), method=c("2009", "2012", "2021"),         lQ.thr=0.6, hQ.thr=0.1, start.month=1, digits=2, fun=NULL, ...,         epsilon.type=c("none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),         epsilon.value=NA)  # S3 method for class 'data.frame' gof(sim, obs, na.rm=TRUE, do.spearman=FALSE, do.pbfdc=FALSE,         j=1, lambda=0.95, norm="sd", s=c(1,1,1), method=c("2009", "2012", "2021"),         lQ.thr=0.6, hQ.thr=0.1, start.month=1, digits=2, fun=NULL, ...,         epsilon.type=c("none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),         epsilon.value=NA)  # S3 method for class 'zoo' gof(sim, obs, na.rm=TRUE, do.spearman=FALSE, do.pbfdc=FALSE,         j=1, lambda=0.95, norm="sd", s=c(1,1,1), method=c("2009", "2012", "2021"),         lQ.thr=0.6, hQ.thr=0.1, start.month=1, digits=2, fun=NULL, ...,         epsilon.type=c("none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),         epsilon.value=NA)

title: Numerical Goodness-of-fit measures — gof
text: sim numeric, zoo, matrix data.frame simulated values obs numeric, zoo, matrix data.frame observed values na.rm logical value indicating whether 'NA' stripped computation proceeds.  'NA' value found -th position obs sim, -th value obs sim removed computation. .spearman logical. Indicates Spearman correlation computed. default FALSE. .pbfdc logical. Indicates Percent Bias Slope midsegment Flow Duration Curve (pbiasfdc) computed. default FALSE. j argument passed mNSE wsNSE functions. lambda argument passed wsNSE function. norm argument passed nrmse function s argument passed KGE, KGElf, sKGE KGEkm functions. method argument passed KGE, KGElf, sKGE KGEkm functions. lQ.thr [OPTIONAL]. used computation pbiasFDC % (pbiasfdc function) weighted seasonal Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency (wsNSE function. hQ.thr [OPTIONAL]. used computation pbiasFDC % (pbiasfdc function), high flow bias (HFB, HFB function) weighted seasonal Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency (wsNSE function. start.month [OPTIONAL]. used computation split KGE (sKGE), annual peak flow bias (APFB) high flow bias (HFB) (hydrological) year interest different calendar year. numeric [1:12] indicating starting month (hydrological) year. Numeric values [1, 12] represent months [January, December]. default start.month=1. digits decimal places used rounding goodness--fit indexes. fun function applied sim obs order obtain transformed values thereof computing goodness--fit functions. first argument MUST numeric vector name (e.g., x), additional arguments passed using .... ... arguments passed fun, addition mandatory first numeric vector. epsilon.type argument used define numeric value added sim obs applying fun.  designed allow use logarithm similar functions work zero values. Valid values epsilon.type : 1) "none": sim obs used FUN without addition nummeric value. 2) "Pushpalatha2012": one hundredth (1/100) mean observed values added sim obs applying FUN, described Pushpalatha et al. (2012). 3) "otherFactor": numeric value defined epsilon.value argument used multiply mean observed values, instead one hundredth (1/100) described Pushpalatha et al. (2012). resulting value added sim obs, applying FUN. 4) "otherValue": numeric value defined epsilon.value argument directly added sim obs, applying FUN. epsilon.value -) epsilon.type="otherValue" represents numeric value added sim obs applying fun.   -) epsilon.type="otherFactor" represents numeric factor used multiply mean observed values, instead one hundredth (1/100) described Pushpalatha et al. (2012). resulting value added sim obs applying fun.

title: Numerical Goodness-of-fit measures — gof
text: output gof function matrix one column , following rows: Mean Error MAE Mean Absolute Error MSE Mean Squared Error RMSE Root Mean Square Error ubRMSE Unbiased Root Mean Square Error NRMSE Normalized Root Mean Square Error  ( -100% <= NRMSE <= 100% ) PBIAS Percent Bias ( -Inf <= PBIAS <= Inf [%] ) RSR Ratio RMSE Standard Deviation Observations, RSR = rms / sd(obs). ( 0 <= RSR <= +Inf ) rSD Ratio Standard Deviations, rSD = sd(sim) / sd(obs) NSE Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency ( -Inf <= NSE <= 1 ) mNSE Modified Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency ( -Inf <= mNSE <= 1 ) rNSE Relative Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency ( -Inf <= rNSE <= 1 ) wNSE Weighted Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency ( -Inf <= wNSE <= 1 ) wsNSE Weighted Seasonal Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency ( -Inf <= wsNSE <= 1 ) d Index Agreement ( 0 <= d <= 1 ) dr Refined Index Agreement ( -1 <= dr <= 1 ) md Modified Index Agreement ( 0 <= md <= 1 ) rd Relative Index Agreement ( 0 <= rd <= 1 ) cp Persistence Index ( 0 <= cp <= 1 ) r Pearson Correlation coefficient ( -1 <= r <= 1 ) R2 Coefficient Determination ( 0 <= R2 <= 1 ) bR2 R2 multiplied coefficient regression line sim obs  ( 0 <= bR2 <= 1 ) VE Volumetric efficiency sim obs  ( -Inf <= VE <= 1) KGE Kling-Gupta efficiency sim obs  ( -Inf <= KGE <= 1 ) KGElf Kling-Gupta Efficiency low values sim obs  ( -Inf <= KGElf <= 1 ) KGEnp Non-parametric version Kling-Gupta Efficiency sim obs  ( -Inf <= KGEnp <= 1 ) KGEkm Knowable Moments Kling-Gupta Efficiency sim obs  ( -Inf <= KGEnp <= 1 ) following outputs produced sim obs zoo objects sub-annual temporal frequency: sKGE Split Kling-Gupta Efficiency sim obs  ( -Inf <= sKGE <= 1 ) APFB Annual Peak Flow Bias ( 0 <= APFB <= Inf ) HBF High Flow Bias ( 0 <= HFB <= Inf ) following outputs produced defaul vlaues specific argument changed user: r.Spearman Spearman Correlation coefficient ( -1 <= r.Spearman <= 1 ). computed .spearman=TRUE pbiasfdc PBIAS slope midsegment Flow Duration Curve. computed .pbfdc=FALSE

code: ################## # Example 1: basic ideal case obs <- 1:10 sim <- 1:10 gof(sim, obs) #>         [,1] #> ME         0 #> MAE        0 #> MSE        0 #> RMSE       0 #> ubRMSE     0 #> NRMSE %    0 #> PBIAS %    0 #> RSR        0 #> rSD        1 #> NSE        1 #> mNSE       1 #> rNSE       1 #> wNSE       1 #> wsNSE      1 #> d          1 #> dr         1 #> md         1 #> rd         1 #> cp         1 #> r          1 #> R2         1 #> bR2        1 #> VE         1 #> KGE        0 #> KGElf      0 #> KGEnp      1 #> KGEkm      1  obs <- 1:10 sim <- 2:11 gof(sim, obs) #>          [,1] #> ME       1.00 #> MAE      1.00 #> MSE      1.00 #> RMSE     1.00 #> ubRMSE   0.00 #> NRMSE % 33.00 #> PBIAS % 18.20 #> RSR      0.33 #> rSD      1.00 #> NSE      0.88 #> mNSE     0.60 #> rNSE     0.43 #> wNSE     0.88 #> wsNSE    0.65 #> d        0.97 #> dr       0.80 #> md       0.80 #> rd       0.86 #> cp       0.00 #> r        1.00 #> R2       0.88 #> bR2      0.77 #> VE       0.82 #> KGE     -0.18 #> KGElf   -0.03 #> KGEnp    0.81 #> KGEkm    0.81  ################## # Example 2:  # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Generating a simulated daily time series, initially equal to the observed series sim <- obs   # Computing the 'gof' for the "best" (unattainable) case gof(sim=sim, obs=obs) #>         [,1] #> ME         0 #> MAE        0 #> MSE        0 #> RMSE       0 #> ubRMSE     0 #> NRMSE %    0 #> PBIAS %    0 #> RSR        0 #> rSD        1 #> NSE        1 #> mNSE       1 #> rNSE       1 #> wNSE       1 #> wsNSE      1 #> d          1 #> dr         1 #> md         1 #> rd         1 #> cp         1 #> r          1 #> R2         1 #> bR2        1 #> VE         1 #> KGE        0 #> KGElf      0 #> KGEnp      1 #> KGEkm      1 #> sKGE       0 #> APFB       0 #> HFB        1  ################## # Example 3: gof for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values.  #            This random noise has more relative importance for low flows than  #            for medium and high flows.    # Randomly changing the first 1826 elements of 'sim', by using a normal distribution  # with mean 10 and standard deviation equal to 1 (default of 'rnorm'). sim[1:1826] <- obs[1:1826] + rnorm(1826, mean=10) ggof(sim, obs)   gof(sim=sim, obs=obs) #>          [,1] #> ME       4.98 #> MAE      4.98 #> MSE     50.20 #> RMSE     7.09 #> ubRMSE   5.04 #> NRMSE % 35.40 #> PBIAS % 31.50 #> RSR      0.35 #> rSD      1.03 #> NSE      0.87 #> mNSE     0.61 #> rNSE    -0.55 #> wNSE     0.98 #> wsNSE    0.76 #> d        0.97 #> dr       0.80 #> md       0.80 #> rd       0.63 #> cp       0.47 #> r        0.97 #> R2       0.87 #> bR2      0.78 #> VE       0.68 #> KGE     -0.32 #> KGElf   -0.08 #> KGEnp    0.63 #> KGEkm    0.66 #> sKGE    -0.35 #> APFB     0.03 #> HFB      0.92  ################## # Example 4: gof for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations.  gof(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log) #>          [,1] #> ME       0.42 #> MAE      0.42 #> MSE      0.48 #> RMSE     0.69 #> ubRMSE   0.55 #> NRMSE % 72.00 #> PBIAS % 18.70 #> RSR      0.72 #> rSD      0.88 #> NSE      0.48 #> mNSE     0.48 #> rNSE    -4.40 #> wNSE     0.74 #> wsNSE    0.78 #> d        0.86 #> dr       0.74 #> md       0.74 #> rd      -0.44 #> cp      -7.93 #> r        0.82 #> R2       0.48 #> bR2      0.43 #> VE       0.81 #> KGE     -0.21 #> KGElf   -0.08 #> KGEnp    0.74 #> KGEkm    0.73 #> sKGE    -0.46 #> APFB     0.01 #> HFB      0.98  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim) lobs <- log(obs) gof(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #>          [,1] #> ME       0.42 #> MAE      0.42 #> MSE      0.48 #> RMSE     0.69 #> ubRMSE   0.55 #> NRMSE % 72.00 #> PBIAS % 18.70 #> RSR      0.72 #> rSD      0.88 #> NSE      0.48 #> mNSE     0.48 #> rNSE    -4.40 #> wNSE     0.74 #> wsNSE    0.78 #> d        0.86 #> dr       0.74 #> md       0.74 #> rd      -0.44 #> cp      -7.93 #> r        0.82 #> R2       0.48 #> bR2      0.43 #> VE       0.81 #> KGE     -0.21 #> KGElf   -0.18 #> KGEnp    0.74 #> KGEkm    0.73 #> sKGE    -0.24 #> APFB     0.01 #> HFB      0.98  ################## # Example 5: gof for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding the Pushpalatha2012 constant #            during computations  gof(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="Pushpalatha2012") #>          [,1] #> ME       0.41 #> MAE      0.41 #> MSE      0.46 #> RMSE     0.68 #> ubRMSE   0.54 #> NRMSE % 71.50 #> PBIAS % 18.10 #> RSR      0.72 #> rSD      0.89 #> NSE      0.49 #> mNSE     0.48 #> rNSE    -2.03 #> wNSE     0.74 #> wsNSE    0.78 #> d        0.86 #> dr       0.74 #> md       0.74 #> rd       0.19 #> cp      -7.67 #> r        0.83 #> R2       0.49 #> bR2      0.44 #> VE       0.82 #> KGE     -0.20 #> KGElf   -0.08 #> KGEnp    0.74 #> KGEkm    0.74 #> sKGE    -0.39 #> APFB     0.01 #> HFB      0.98  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 eps  <- mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE)/100 lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) gof(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #>          [,1] #> ME       0.41 #> MAE      0.41 #> MSE      0.46 #> RMSE     0.68 #> ubRMSE   0.54 #> NRMSE % 71.50 #> PBIAS % 18.10 #> RSR      0.72 #> rSD      0.89 #> NSE      0.49 #> mNSE     0.48 #> rNSE    -2.03 #> wNSE     0.74 #> wsNSE    0.78 #> d        0.86 #> dr       0.74 #> md       0.74 #> rd       0.19 #> cp      -7.67 #> r        0.83 #> R2       0.49 #> bR2      0.44 #> VE       0.82 #> KGE     -0.20 #> KGElf   -0.12 #> KGEnp    0.75 #> KGEkm    0.74 #> sKGE    -0.23 #> APFB     0.01 #> HFB      0.98  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ################## # Example 6: gof for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding a user-defined constant #            during computations  eps <- 0.01 gof(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherValue", epsilon.value=eps)  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) gof(sim=lsim, obs=lobs)  ################## # Example 7: gof for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and using a user-defined factor #            to multiply the mean of the observed values to obtain the constant #            to be added to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fact <- 1/50 gof(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherFactor", epsilon.value=fact)  # Verifying the previous value: eps  <- fact*mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE) lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) gof(sim=lsim, obs=lobs)  ################## # Example 8: gof for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying a  #            user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  gof(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1)  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) gof(sim=sim1, obs=obs1)  # Storing a matrix object with all the GoFs: g <-  gof(sim, obs)  # Getting only the RMSE g[4,1] g["RMSE",]   # Writing all the GoFs into a TXT file write.table(g, "GoFs.txt", col.names=FALSE, quote=FALSE)  # Getting the graphical representation of 'obs' and 'sim' along with the  # numeric goodness of fit  ggof(sim=sim, obs=obs) } # }

title: Goodness-of-fit (GoF) functions for numerical and graphical comparison of simulated and observed time series, mainly focused on hydrological modelling. — hydroGOF-package
code: obs <- 1:100 sim <- obs  # Numerical goodness of fit gof(sim,obs) #>         [,1] #> ME         0 #> MAE        0 #> MSE        0 #> RMSE       0 #> ubRMSE     0 #> NRMSE %    0 #> PBIAS %    0 #> RSR        0 #> rSD        1 #> NSE        1 #> mNSE       1 #> rNSE       1 #> wNSE       1 #> wsNSE      1 #> d          1 #> dr         1 #> md         1 #> rd         1 #> cp         1 #> r          1 #> R2         1 #> bR2        1 #> VE         1 #> KGE        0 #> KGElf      0 #> KGEnp      1 #> KGEkm      1  # Reverting the order of simulated values sim <- 100:1 gof(sim,obs) #>             [,1] #> ME          0.00 #> MAE        50.00 #> MSE      3333.00 #> RMSE       57.73 #> ubRMSE     57.73 #> NRMSE %   199.00 #> PBIAS %     0.00 #> RSR         1.99 #> rSD         1.00 #> NSE        -3.00 #> mNSE       -1.00 #> rNSE     -457.56 #> wNSE       -3.00 #> wsNSE      -1.00 #> d           0.00 #> dr          0.00 #> md          0.00 #> rd       -113.64 #> cp      -3266.67 #> r          -1.00 #> R2         -3.00 #> bR2        -1.52 #> VE          0.01 #> KGE        -1.24 #> KGElf      -0.87 #> KGEnp      -1.00 #> KGEkm      -1.00  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ggof(sim, obs) } # }  ################## # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 require(zoo) data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Generating a simulated daily time series, initially equal to observations sim <- obs   # Getting the numeric goodness-of-fit measures for the "best" (unattainable) case gof(sim=sim, obs=obs) #>         [,1] #> ME         0 #> MAE        0 #> MSE        0 #> RMSE       0 #> ubRMSE     0 #> NRMSE %    0 #> PBIAS %    0 #> RSR        0 #> rSD        1 #> NSE        1 #> mNSE       1 #> rNSE       1 #> wNSE       1 #> wsNSE      1 #> d          1 #> dr         1 #> md         1 #> rd         1 #> cp         1 #> r          1 #> R2         1 #> bR2        1 #> VE         1 #> KGE        0 #> KGElf      0 #> KGEnp      1 #> KGEkm      1 #> sKGE       0 #> APFB       0 #> HFB        1  # Randomly changing the first 2000 elements of 'sim', by using a normal  # distribution  with mean 10 and standard deviation equal to 1 (default of 'rnorm'). sim[1:2000] <- obs[1:2000] + rnorm(2000, mean=10)  # Getting the new numeric goodness of fit gof(sim=sim, obs=obs) #>          [,1] #> ME       5.46 #> MAE      5.46 #> MSE     55.05 #> RMSE     7.42 #> ubRMSE   5.02 #> NRMSE % 37.10 #> PBIAS % 34.50 #> RSR      0.37 #> rSD      1.04 #> NSE      0.86 #> mNSE     0.57 #> rNSE    -0.56 #> wNSE     0.97 #> wsNSE    0.72 #> d        0.97 #> dr       0.78 #> md       0.78 #> rd       0.63 #> cp       0.42 #> r        0.97 #> R2       0.86 #> bR2      0.76 #> VE       0.65 #> KGE     -0.35 #> KGElf   -0.09 #> KGEnp    0.61 #> KGEkm    0.63 #> sKGE    -0.37 #> APFB     0.03 #> HFB      0.92  # Graphical representation of 'obs' vs 'sim', along with the numeric  # goodness-of-fit measures if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ ggof(sim=sim, obs=obs) } # }

title: Mean Absolute Error — mae
code: mae(sim, obs, ...)  # Default S3 method mae(sim, obs, na.rm=TRUE, fun=NULL, ...,               epsilon.type=c("none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),               epsilon.value=NA)  # S3 method for class 'data.frame' mae(sim, obs, na.rm=TRUE, fun=NULL, ...,               epsilon.type=c("none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),               epsilon.value=NA)  # S3 method for class 'matrix' mae(sim, obs, na.rm=TRUE, fun=NULL, ...,               epsilon.type=c("none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),               epsilon.value=NA)  # S3 method for class 'zoo' mae(sim, obs, na.rm=TRUE, fun=NULL, ...,               epsilon.type=c("none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),               epsilon.value=NA)

title: Mean Absolute Error — mae
text: sim numeric, zoo, matrix data.frame simulated values obs numeric, zoo, matrix data.frame observed values na.rm logical value indicating whether 'NA' stripped computation proceeds.  'NA' value found -th position obs sim, -th value obs sim removed computation. fun function applied sim obs order obtain transformed values thereof computing goodness--fit index. first argument MUST numeric vector name (e.g., x), additional arguments passed using .... ... arguments passed fun, addition mandatory first numeric vector. epsilon.type argument used define numeric value added sim obs applying fun.  designed allow use logarithm similar functions work zero values. Valid values epsilon.type : 1) "none": sim obs used fun without addition numeric value. default option. 2) "Pushpalatha2012": one hundredth (1/100) mean observed values added sim obs applying fun, described Pushpalatha et al. (2012). 3) "otherFactor": numeric value defined epsilon.value argument used multiply mean observed values, instead one hundredth (1/100) described Pushpalatha et al. (2012). resulting value added sim obs, applying fun. 4) "otherValue": numeric value defined epsilon.value argument directly added sim obs, applying fun. epsilon.value -) epsilon.type="otherValue" represents numeric value added sim obs applying fun.   -) epsilon.type="otherFactor" represents numeric factor used multiply mean observed values, instead one hundredth (1/100) described Pushpalatha et al. (2012). resulting value added sim obs applying fun.

title: Mean Absolute Error — mae
text: obs sim length/dimension missing values obs sim removed computation proceeds, positions non-missing values obs sim considered computation

title: Mean Absolute Error — mae
code: ################## # Example 1: basic ideal case obs <- 1:10 sim <- 1:10 mae(sim, obs) #> [1] 0  obs <- 1:10 sim <- 2:11 mae(sim, obs) #> [1] 1  ################## # Example 2:  # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Generating a simulated daily time series, initially equal to the observed series sim <- obs   # Computing the 'mae' for the "best" (unattainable) case mae(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0  ################## # Example 3: mae for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values.  #            This random noise has more relative importance for ow flows than  #            for medium and high flows.    # Randomly changing the first 1826 elements of 'sim', by using a normal distribution  # with mean 10 and standard deviation equal to 1 (default of 'rnorm'). sim[1:1826] <- obs[1:1826] + rnorm(1826, mean=10) ggof(sim, obs)   mae(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 5.007622  ################## # Example 4: mae for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations.  mae(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log) #> [1] 0.4237196  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim) lobs <- log(obs) mae(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.4237196  ################## # Example 5: mae for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding the Pushpalatha2012 constant #            during computations  mae(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="Pushpalatha2012") #> [1] 0.414968  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 eps  <- mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE)/100 lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) mae(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.414968  ################## # Example 6: mae for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding a user-defined constant #            during computations  eps <- 0.01 mae(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherValue", epsilon.value=eps) #> [1] 0.4231498  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) mae(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.4231498  ################## # Example 7: mae for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and using a user-defined factor #            to multiply the mean of the observed values to obtain the constant #            to be added to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fact <- 1/50 mae(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherFactor", epsilon.value=fact) #> [1] 0.4067257  # Verifying the previous value: eps  <- fact*mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE) lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) mae(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.4067257  ################## # Example 8: mae for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying a  #            user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  mae(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1) #> [1] 0.6505247  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) mae(sim=sim1, obs=obs1) #> [1] 0.6505247

title: Modified Index of Agreement — md
code: md(sim, obs, ...)  # Default S3 method md(sim, obs, j=1, na.rm=TRUE, fun=NULL, ...,             epsilon.type=c("none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),              epsilon.value=NA)  # S3 method for class 'data.frame' md(sim, obs, j=1, na.rm=TRUE, fun=NULL, ...,             epsilon.type=c("none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),              epsilon.value=NA)  # S3 method for class 'matrix' md(sim, obs, j=1, na.rm=TRUE, fun=NULL, ...,             epsilon.type=c("none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),              epsilon.value=NA)  # S3 method for class 'zoo' md(sim, obs, j=1, na.rm=TRUE, fun=NULL, ...,             epsilon.type=c("none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),              epsilon.value=NA)

title: Modified Index of Agreement — md
text: sim numeric, zoo, matrix data.frame simulated values obs numeric, zoo, matrix data.frame observed values j numeric, exponent used computation modified index agreement. default value j=1. na.rm logical value indicating whether 'NA' stripped computation proceeds.  'NA' value found -th position obs sim, -th value obs sim removed computation. fun function applied sim obs order obtain transformed values thereof computing modified index agreement. first argument MUST numeric vector name (e.g., x), additional arguments passed using .... ... arguments passed fun, addition mandatory first numeric vector. epsilon.type argument used define numeric value added sim obs applying fun.  designed allow use logarithm similar functions work zero values. Valid values epsilon.type : 1) "none": sim obs used fun without addition nummeric value. 2) "Pushpalatha2012": one hundredth (1/100) mean observed values added sim obs applying fun, described Pushpalatha et al. (2012). 3) "otherFactor": numeric value defined epsilon.value argument used multiply mean observed values, instead one hundredth (1/100) described Pushpalatha et al. (2012). resulting value added sim obs, applying fun. 4) "otherValue": numeric value defined epsilon.value argument directly added sim obs, applying fun. epsilon.value numeric value added sim obs epsilon.type="otherValue".

title: Modified Index of Agreement — md
text: $$md = 1 - \frac{ \sum_{=1}^N {\left| O_i - S_i \right| ^j} } { \sum_{=1}^N { \left| S_i -  \bar{O} \right| + \left| O_i -  \bar{O}  \right|^j } } $$ Index Agreement (d) developed Willmott (1981) standardized measure degree model prediction error varies 0 1.  value 1 indicates perfect match, 0 indicates agreement (Willmott, 1981). index agreement can detect additive proportional differences observed simulated means variances; however, overly sensitive extreme values due squared differences (Legates McCabe, 1999).

(1981). validation models. Physical Geography, 2, 184–194. doi:10.1080/02723646.1981.10642213. Willmott, C.J. (1984). evaluation model performance physical geography. Spatial Statistics Models, G. L. (1985), Statistics Evaluation Comparison Models, J. Geophys. Res., 90(C5), 8995-9005. doi:10.1029/JC090iC05p08995. Legates, D.R.; McCabe, G. J. code: ################## # Example 1: basic ideal case obs <- 1:10 sim <- 1:10 me(sim, obs) #> [1] 0  obs <- 1:10 sim <- 2:11 me(sim, obs) #> [1] 1  ################## # Example 2:  # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Generating a simulated daily time series, initially equal to the observed series sim <- obs   # Computing the 'me' for the "best" (unattainable) case me(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0  ################## # Example 3: me for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values.  #            This random noise has more relative importance for ow flows than  #            for medium and high flows.    # Randomly changing the first 1826 elements of 'sim', by using a normal distribution  # with mean 10 and standard deviation equal to 1 (default of 'rnorm'). sim[1:1826] <- obs[1:1826] + rnorm(1826, mean=10) ggof(sim, obs)   me(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 4.994078  ################## # Example 4: me for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations.  me(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log) #> [1] 0.422733  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim) lobs <- log(obs) me(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.422733  ################## # Example 5: me for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding the Pushpalatha2012 constant #            during computations  me(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="Pushpalatha2012") #> [1] 0.4139926  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 eps  <- mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE)/100 lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) me(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.4139926  ################## # Example 6: me for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding a user-defined constant #            during computations  eps <- 0.01 me(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherValue", epsilon.value=eps) #> [1] 0.4221638  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) me(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.4221638  ################## # Example 7: me for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and using a user-defined factor #            to multiply the mean of the observed values to obtain the constant #            to be added to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fact <- 1/50 me(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherFactor", epsilon.value=fact) #> [1] 0.4057612  # Verifying the previous value: eps  <- fact*mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE) lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) me(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.4057612  ################## # Example 8: me for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying a  #            user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  me(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1) #> [1] 0.6487607  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) me(sim=sim1, obs=obs1) #> [1] 0.6487607

Valid values :  -) sd    : standard deviation observations (default).  -) maxmin: difference maximum minimum observed values -) mean  : arithmetic mean observed values -) IQR   : interquantile range observed values, computed using stats::IQR function type=7. fun function applied sim obs order obtain transformed values thereof computing goodness--fit index. first argument MUST numeric vector name (e.g., x), additional arguments passed using .... ... arguments passed fun, addition mandatory first numeric vector. epsilon.type argument used define numeric value added sim obs applying fun.  designed allow use logarithm similar functions work zero values. Valid values epsilon.type : 1) "none": sim obs used fun without addition numeric value. default option. 2) "Pushpalatha2012": one hundredth (1/100) mean observed values added sim obs applying fun, described Pushpalatha et al. (2012). 3) "otherFactor": numeric value defined epsilon.value argument used multiply mean observed values, instead one hundredth (1/100) described Pushpalatha et al. (2012). resulting value added sim obs, applying fun. 4) "otherValue": numeric value defined epsilon.value argument directly added sim obs, applying fun. epsilon.value -) epsilon.type="otherValue" represents numeric value added sim obs applying fun.   -) epsilon.type="otherFactor" represents numeric factor used multiply mean observed values, instead one hundredth (1/100) described Pushpalatha et al. (2012). resulting value added sim obs applying fun.

title: Normalized Root Mean Square Error — nrmse
text: $$ nrmse = 100 \frac {\sqrt{ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{=1}^N { \left( S_i - O_i \right)^2 } } } {nval} $$ $$nval=  \left\{   \begin{array}{cl}     sd(O_i)           & , \: \textrm{norm="sd"} \\     O_{max} - O_{min} & , \: \textrm{norm="maxmin"}   \end{array}   \right.$$

title: Normalized Root Mean Square Error — nrmse
text: Mauricio Zambrano Bigiarini <mzb.devel@gmail.com>

title: Normalized Root Mean Square Error — nrmse
text: obs sim length/dimension Missing values obs sim removed computation proceeds, positions non-missing values obs sim considered computation

title: Normalized Root Mean Square Error — nrmse
code: ################## # Example 1: basic ideal case obs <- 1:10 sim <- 1:10 nrmse(sim, obs) #> [1] 0  obs <- 1:10 sim <- 2:11 nrmse(sim, obs) #> [1] 33  ################## # Example 2:  # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Generating a simulated daily time series, initially equal to the observed series sim <- obs   # Computing the 'nrmse' for the "best" (unattainable) case nrmse(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0  ################## # Example 3: nrmse for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values.  #            This random noise has more relative importance for ow flows than  #            for medium and high flows.    # Randomly changing the first 1826 elements of 'sim', by using a normal distribution  # with mean 10 and standard deviation equal to 1 (default of 'rnorm'). sim[1:1826] <- obs[1:1826] + rnorm(1826, mean=10) ggof(sim, obs)   nrmse(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 35.5  ################## # Example 4: nrmse for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations.  nrmse(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log) #> [1] 72.2  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim) lobs <- log(obs) nrmse(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 72.2  ################## # Example 5: nrmse for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding the Pushpalatha2012 constant #            during computations  nrmse(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="Pushpalatha2012") #> [1] 71.7  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 eps  <- mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE)/100 lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) nrmse(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 71.7  ################## # Example 6: nrmse for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding a user-defined constant #            during computations  eps <- 0.01 nrmse(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherValue", epsilon.value=eps) #> [1] 72.2  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) nrmse(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 72.2  ################## # Example 7: nrmse for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and using a user-defined factor #            to multiply the mean of the observed values to obtain the constant #            to be added to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fact <- 1/50 nrmse(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherFactor", epsilon.value=fact) #> [1] 71.3  # Verifying the previous value: eps  <- fact*mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE) lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) nrmse(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 71.3  ################## # Example 8: nrmse for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying a  #            user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  nrmse(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1) #> [1] 52.4  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) nrmse(sim=sim1, obs=obs1) #> [1] 52.4

title: Percent Bias — pbias
text: $$PBIAS = 100 \frac{  \sum_{=1}^N { \left( S_i - O_i \right) } } { \sum_{=1}^N O_i} $$ Percent bias (PBIAS) measures average tendency simulated values larger smaller observed ones. optimal value PBIAS 0.0, low-magnitude values indicating accurate model simulation. Positive values indicate overestimation bias, whereas negative values indicate model underestimation bias

title: P-factor — pfactor
text: P-factor percent observations within given uncertainty bounds. Ideally, .e., combination model structure parameter values perfectly represents catchment study, absence measurement errors additional sources uncertainty, simulated values perfect match observations, leading P-factor equal 1, R-factor equal zero. However, real-world applications aim encompassing much observations possible within given uncertainty bounds (P-factor close 1) keeping width uncertainty bounds small possible (R-factor close 0), order avoid obtaining good bracketing observations expense uncertainty bounds wide informative decision-making process.

title: P-factor — pfactor
code: pfactor(x, ...)  # Default S3 method pfactor(x, lband, uband, na.rm=TRUE, ...)  # S3 method for class 'data.frame' pfactor(x, lband, uband, na.rm=TRUE, ...)  # S3 method for class 'matrix' pfactor(x, lband, uband, na.rm=TRUE, ...)

title: P-factor — pfactor
text: x ts zoo object observed values. lband numeric, ts zoo object values lower uncertainty bound uband numeric, ts zoo object values upper uncertainty bound na.rm logical value indicating whether 'NA' values stripped computation proceeds. ... arguments passed methods.

title: P-factor — pfactor
text: P-factor quantifies percentage observed values fall within prediction uncertainty band defined lower upper bounds. measure coverage uncertainty interval. Mathematically, P-factor defined : $$   P\text{-factor} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{=1}^{N} \left( lband_i \le x_i \le uband_i \right) $$ \(N\) total number observations, \(x_i\) observed value time step \(\), \(lband_i\) \(uband_i\) lower upper uncertainty bounds, respectively. function \((\cdot)\) indicator function takes value 1 observed value lies within uncertainty bounds 0 otherwise. P-factor ranges 0 1. value 1 indicates observations bracketed uncertainty bounds, whereas value 0 indicates none observations fall within bounds.

title: P-factor — pfactor
text: Percent x observations within given uncertainty bounds given lband uband. x, lband, uband matrices, returned value vector P-factor computed column.

code: x <- 1:10 lband <- x - 0.1 uband <- x + 0.1 pfactor(x, lband, uband) #> [1] 1  lband <- x - rnorm(10) uband <- x + rnorm(10) pfactor(x, lband, uband) #> [1] 0.1  ############# # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Selecting only the daily values belonging to the year 1961 obs <- window(obs, end=as.Date("1961-12-31"))  # Generating the lower and upper uncertainty bounds, centred at the observations lband <- obs - 5 uband <- obs + 5  pfactor(obs, lband, uband) #> [1] 1  # Randomly generating the lower and upper uncertainty bounds uband <- obs + rnorm(length(obs)) lband <- obs - rnorm(length(obs))  pfactor(obs, lband, uband) #> [1] 0.2383562

title: Plotting 2 Time Series — plot2
code: sim <- 2:11 obs <- 1:10 if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ plot2(sim, obs) } # }  ################## # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Generating a simulated daily time series, initially equal to the observed series sim <- obs   # Randomly changing the first 2000 elements of 'sim', by using a normal distribution  # with mean 10 and standard deviation equal to 1 (default of 'rnorm'). sim[1:2000] <- obs[1:2000] + rnorm(2000, mean=10)  # Plotting 'sim' and 'obs' in 2 separate panels plot2(x=obs, y=sim)   # Plotting 'sim' and 'obs' in the same window plot2(x=obs, y=sim, plot.type="single")

title: Pearson correlation coefficient — rPearson
code: rPearson(sim, obs, ...)  # Default S3 method rPearson(sim, obs, fun=NULL, ...,               epsilon.type=c("none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),               epsilon.value=NA)  # S3 method for class 'matrix' rPearson(sim, obs, na.rm=TRUE, fun=NULL, ...,               epsilon.type=c("none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),               epsilon.value=NA)  # S3 method for class 'data.frame' rPearson(sim, obs, na.rm=TRUE, fun=NULL, ...,               epsilon.type=c("none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),               epsilon.value=NA)  # S3 method for class 'zoo' rPearson(sim, obs, na.rm=TRUE, fun=NULL, ...,               epsilon.type=c("none", "Pushpalatha2012", "otherFactor", "otherValue"),               epsilon.value=NA)

title: Pearson correlation coefficient — rPearson
text: sim numeric, zoo, matrix data.frame simulated values obs numeric, zoo, matrix data.frame observed values na.rm logical value indicating whether 'NA' stripped computation proceeds.  'NA' value found -th position obs sim, -th value obs sim removed computation. fun function applied sim obs order obtain transformed values thereof computing goodness--fit index. first argument MUST numeric vector name (e.g., x), additional arguments passed using .... ... arguments passed fun, addition mandatory first numeric vector. epsilon.type argument used define numeric value added sim obs applying fun.  designed allow use logarithm similar functions work zero values. Valid values epsilon.type : 1) "none": sim obs used fun without addition numeric value. default option. 2) "Pushpalatha2012": one hundredth (1/100) mean observed values added sim obs applying fun, described Pushpalatha et al. (2012). 3) "otherFactor": numeric value defined epsilon.value argument used multiply mean observed values, instead one hundredth (1/100) described Pushpalatha et al. (2012). resulting value added sim obs, applying fun. 4) "otherValue": numeric value defined epsilon.value argument directly added sim obs, applying fun. epsilon.value numeric value added sim obs epsilon.type="otherValue".

title: Pearson correlation coefficient — rPearson
text: wrapper cor function. Pearson correlation coefficient (PCC) correlation coefficient measures linear correlation two sets data. ratio covariance two variables product standard deviations; thus, essentially normalized measurement covariance, result always value -1 1. covariance , measure can reflect linear correlation variables, ignores many types relationships correlations. correlation coefficient ranges -1 1. absolute value exactly 1 implies linear equation describes relationship sim obs perfectly, data points lying line. correlation sign determined regression slope: value +1 implies data points lie line sim  increases obs  increases, vice versa -1. value 0 implies linear dependency variables.

title: Pearson correlation coefficient — rPearson
text: Pearson correlation coefficient sim obs. sim obs matrixes, returned value vector, Pearson correlation coefficient column sim obs.

title: Pearson correlation coefficient — rPearson
text: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pearson_correlation_coefficient Pearson, K. (1920). Notes history correlation. Biometrika, 13(1), 25-45. doi:10.2307/2331722. Schober, P.; Boer, C.; Schwarte, L.. (2018). Correlation coefficients: appropriate use interpretation. Anesthesia Analgesia, 126(5), 1763-1768. doi:10.1213/ANE.0000000000002864.

title: Pearson correlation coefficient — rPearson
text: Mauricio Zambrano Bigiarini <mzb.devel@gmail.com>

title: Pearson correlation coefficient — rPearson
text: obs sim length/dimension missing values obs sim removed computation proceeds, positions non-missing values obs sim considered computation

title: Pearson correlation coefficient — rPearson
code: ################## # Example 1: basic ideal case obs <- 1:10 sim <- 1:10 rPearson(sim, obs) #> [1] 1  obs <- 1:10 sim <- 2:11 rPearson(sim, obs) #> [1] 1  ################## # Example 2:  # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Generating a simulated daily time series, initially equal to the observed series sim <- obs   # Computing the 'rPearson' for the "best" (unattainable) case rPearson(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 1  ################## # Example 3: rPearson for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values.  #            This random noise has more relative importance for ow flows than  #            for medium and high flows.    # Randomly changing the first 1826 elements of 'sim', by using a normal distribution  # with mean 10 and standard deviation equal to 1 (default of 'rnorm'). sim[1:1826] <- obs[1:1826] + rnorm(1826, mean=10) ggof(sim, obs)   rPearson(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.9697702  ################## # Example 4: rPearson for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations.  rPearson(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log) #> [1] 0.8216426  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim) lobs <- log(obs) rPearson(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.8216426  ################## # Example 5: rPearson for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding the Pushpalatha2012 constant #            during computations  rPearson(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="Pushpalatha2012") #> [1] 0.8253328  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 eps  <- mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE)/100 lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) rPearson(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.8253328  ################## # Example 6: rPearson for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding a user-defined constant #            during computations  eps <- 0.01 rPearson(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherValue", epsilon.value=eps) #> [1] 0.8218858  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) rPearson(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.8218858  ################## # Example 7: rPearson for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and using a user-defined factor #            to multiply the mean of the observed values to obtain the constant #            to be added to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fact <- 1/50 rPearson(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherFactor", epsilon.value=fact) #> [1] 0.8287287  # Verifying the previous value: eps  <- fact*mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE) lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) rPearson(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.8287287  ################## # Example 8: rPearson for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying a  #            user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  rPearson(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1) #> [1] 0.9238733  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) rPearson(sim=sim1, obs=obs1) #> [1] 0.9238733

title: Spearman's rank correlation coefficient — rSpearman
text: wrapper cor function. Spearman's rank correlation coefficient nonparametric measure rank correlation (statistical dependence rankings two variables). assesses well relationship two variables can described using monotonic function. Spearman correlation two variables equal Pearson correlation rank values two variables. However, Pearson's correlation assesses linear relationships, Spearman's correlation assesses monotonic relationships (whether linear ). repeated data values, perfect Spearman correlation +1 -1 occurs variables perfect monotone function .

           to multiply the mean of the observed values to obtain the constant #            to be added to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fact <- 1/50 rSpearman(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherFactor", epsilon.value=fact) #> [1] 0.8350614  # Verifying the previous value: eps  <- fact*mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE) lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) rSpearman(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.8350614  ################## # Example 8: rSpearman for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying a  #            user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  rSpearman(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1) #> [1] 0.8350614  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) rSpearman(sim=sim1, obs=obs1) #> [1] 0.8350614

title: Relative Index of Agreement — rd
text: $$rd = 1 - \frac{ \sum_{=1}^N { \left( \frac{O_i - S_i}{O_i} \right) ^2} } { \sum_{=1}^N {  \left( \frac{ \left| S_i -  \bar{O} \right| + \left| O_i -  \bar{O}  \right|}{\bar{O}} \right)^2 } } $$   varies 0 1. value 1 indicates perfect match, 0 indicates agreement .

code: ################## # Example 1: basic ideal case obs <- 1:10 sim <- 1:10 rd(sim, obs) #> [1] 1  obs <- 1:10 sim <- 2:11 rd(sim, obs) #> [1] 0.8625206  ################## # Example 2:  # Loading daily streamflows of the Ega River (Spain), from 1961 to 1970 data(EgaEnEstellaQts) obs <- EgaEnEstellaQts  # Generating a simulated daily time series, initially equal to the observed series sim <- obs   # Computing the 'rd' for the "best" (unattainable) case rd(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 1  ################## # Example 3: rd for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values.  #            This random noise has more relative importance for ow flows than  #            for medium and high flows.    # Randomly changing the first 1826 elements of 'sim', by using a normal distribution  # with mean 10 and standard deviation equal to 1 (default of 'rnorm'). sim[1:1826] <- obs[1:1826] + rnorm(1826, mean=10) ggof(sim, obs)   rd(sim=sim, obs=obs) #> [1] 0.6257483  ################## # Example 4: rd for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations.  rd(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log) #> [1] -0.4378264  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim) lobs <- log(obs) rd(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] -0.4378264  ################## # Example 5: rd for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding the Pushpalatha2012 constant #            during computations  rd(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="Pushpalatha2012") #> [1] 0.1939582  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 eps  <- mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE)/100 lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) rd(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.1939582  ################## # Example 6: rd for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and adding a user-defined constant #            during computations  eps <- 0.01 rd(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherValue", epsilon.value=eps) #> [1] -0.3509093  # Verifying the previous value: lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) rd(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] -0.3509093  ################## # Example 7: rd for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying (natural)  #            logarithm to 'sim' and 'obs' and using a user-defined factor #            to multiply the mean of the observed values to obtain the constant #            to be added to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fact <- 1/50 rd(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=log, epsilon.type="otherFactor", epsilon.value=fact) #> [1] 0.3871943  # Verifying the previous value: eps  <- fact*mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE) lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) rd(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.3871943  ################## # Example 8: rd for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying a  #            user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  rd(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1) #> [1] 0.8333152  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) rd(sim=sim1, obs=obs1) #> [1] 0.8333152

title: R-factor — rfactor
text: R-factor represents average width given uncertainty bounds divided standard deviation observations. Ideally, .e., combination model structure parameter values perfectly represents catchment study, absence measurement errors additional sources uncertainty, simulated values perfect match observations, leading P-factor equal 1, R-factor equal zero. However, real-world applications aim encompassing much observations possible within given uncertainty bounds (P-factor close 1) keeping width uncertainty bounds small possible (R-factor close 0), order avoid obtaining good bracketing observations expense uncertainty bounds wide informative decision-making process.

title: R-factor — rfactor

title: R-factor — rfactor
text: x ts zoo object observed values. lband numeric, ts zoo object values lower uncertainty bound uband numeric, ts zoo object values upper uncertainty bound na.rm logical value indicating whether 'NA' values stripped computation proceeds. ... arguments passed methods.

title: R-factor — rfactor
text: R-factor quantifies average width prediction uncertainty band relative variability observed data. measure magnitude predictive uncertainty associated model simulation. Mathematically, R-factor defined : $$ R\text{-factor} = \frac{\overline{d_x}}{\sigma_x} $$ \(\sigma_x\) standard deviation observed variable \(x\), \(\overline{d_x}\) average thickness uncertainty band, computed : $$ \overline{d_x} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{=1}^{N} \left( uband_i - lband_i \right) $$ \(N\) total number observations, \(lband_i\) \(uband_i\) lower upper uncertainty bounds, respectively, time step \(\). R-factor ranges 0 infinity, optimal value 0 indicating perfect agreement simulated observed values (.e., zero prediction uncertainty). practical applications, R-factor represents width uncertainty interval small possible. Values close smaller 1 commonly considered indicative acceptable level predictive uncertainty, although acceptable thresholds depend quality observations modeling context. larger fraction observations can often bracketed widening uncertainty bounds, R-factor typically interpreted jointly P-factor. balance high P-factor (good coverage) low R-factor (narrow uncertainty bounds) therefore sought model calibration uncertainty analysis.

Verifying the previous value: eps  <- fact*mean(obs, na.rm=TRUE) lsim <- log(sim+eps) lobs <- log(obs+eps) rsr(sim=lsim, obs=lobs) #> [1] 0.711959  ################## # Example 8: rsr for simulated values equal to observations plus random noise  #            on the first half of the observed values and applying a  #            user-defined function to 'sim' and 'obs' during computations  fun1 <- function(x) {sqrt(x+1)}  rsr(sim=sim, obs=obs, fun=fun1) #> [1] 0.523825  # Verifying the previous value, with the epsilon value following Pushpalatha2012 sim1 <- sqrt(sim+1) obs1 <- sqrt(obs+1) rsr(sim=sim1, obs=obs1) #> [1] 0.523825

title: Split Kling-Gupta Efficiency — sKGE
text: Split Kling-Gupta Efficiency (sKGE) computed dividing time series individual years (hydrological years start.month != 1) calculating Kling-Gupta Efficiency (KGE) year separately. final sKGE value defined arithmetic mean annual KGE values: $$ sKGE = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{y=1}^{N} KGE_y $$ \(N\) number years evaluation period \(KGE_y\) Kling-Gupta Efficiency computed using simulated observed values belonging year \(y\). Kling-Gupta Efficiency defined : $$ KGE = 1 - \sqrt{(r - 1)^2 + (V - 1)^2 + (B - 1)^2} $$ \(r\) linear correlation coefficient simulated observed values, \(V\) represents variability component, \(B\) represents bias component. definitions variability bias components depend selected method argument: method = "2009" (Gupta et al., 2009) $$ \alpha = \frac{\sigma_{sim}}{\sigma_{obs}} $$ $$ \beta = \frac{\mu_{sim}}{\mu_{obs}} $$ variability component \(V=\alpha\) bias component \(B=\beta\). method = "2012" (Kling et al., 2012) $$ \gamma = \frac{CV_{sim}}{CV_{obs}} $$ $$ \beta = \frac{\mu_{sim}}{\mu_{obs}} $$ \(CV = \sigma / \mu\), variability component \(V=\gamma\), bias component \(B=\beta\). method = "2021" (Tang et al., 2021) $$ \alpha = \frac{\sigma_{sim}}{\sigma_{obs}} $$ $$ \beta = \frac{\mu_{sim} - \mu_{obs}}{\sigma_{obs}} $$ variability component \(V=\alpha\) bias component standardized bias term. splitting procedure allows model performance evaluated annual scale, providing measure summarizes inter-annual performance reducing influence temporal aggregation long periods. .PerYear=TRUE, individual annual KGE values also returned. optimal value sKGE 1. Similar traditional Kling-Gupta efficiency, sKGE values can range \(-\infty\) 1, values closer 1 indicate better agreement simulated observed values.

title: Split Kling-Gupta Efficiency — sKGE
